docs.title: Poquoson Fire & Rescue Protocol Dashboard
docs.text: Welcome to the digital protocol library. This system is designed for Conscious Vigilance — getting you the exact information you need on a call with zero friction.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text: Resource Status Medications & Calculators ✅ Live Fire & Rescue SOPs 🚧 Coming Soon Apparatus Checks 🚧 Coming Soon City Policies 🚧 Coming Soon

docs.title: How to Use This Site
docs.text:
• Search: Use the search bar at the top to instantly find any protocol, medication, or SOP.
  • Offline: Once loaded, this site works with zero cell service — in basements, rural scenes, or steel buildings.
  • Mobile: Add to your home screen on iOS or Android for instant access like a native app.

docs.title: Submit an Update or Correction
docs.text:
If you notice a discrepancy or have input for a protocol, do not edit this page directly.

Submit all corrections or suggestions through your department chain of command. Updates are reviewed and integrated during daily maintenance.

Last Updated

This site is actively maintained. If you encounter outdated information, report it immediately.

docs.title: Cardiac Arrest
docs.text: Medication Dose Route Notes Epinephrine 1:10,000 1 mg IV/IO Every 3–5 min Amiodarone 300 mg (1st dose) IV/IO push 150 mg for 2nd dose Lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg IV/IO Alternative to amiodarone Sodium Bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg IV/IO Suspected hyperkalemia or TCA OD Calcium Chloride (10%) 1 g (10 mL) IV/IO Hyperkalemia, calcium-channel blocker OD

docs.title: Symptomatic Bradycardia
docs.text: Medication Dose Route Notes Atropine 0.5 mg IV Repeat q3–5 min; max 3 mg Dopamine 2–20 mcg/kg/min IV infusion Titrate to effect Epinephrine 2–10 mcg/min IV infusion Refractory bradycardia

docs.title: Tachycardia
docs.text: Medication Dose Route Notes Adenosine 6 mg (1st dose) Rapid IV push 12 mg for 2nd/3rd dose; SVT Amiodarone 150 mg over 10 min IV Stable VT with pulse Synchronized Cardioversion 100–200 J — Unstable tachycardia

docs.title: Analgesia & Sedation
docs.text: Medication Dose Route Notes Morphine 2–4 mg IV/IM Titrate; reassess pain Fentanyl 1–2 mcg/kg IV/IN Preferred in hemodynamic instability Ketamine 1–2 mg/kg IV Dissociative; airway maintained Midazolam 2–5 mg IV/IM/IN Seizures, sedation

docs.title: Airway
docs.text: Medication Dose Route Notes Succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg IV/IO RSI paralytic; avoid in hyperkalemia Rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg IV/IO RSI alternative; reversal with sugammadex Etomidate 0.3 mg/kg IV RSI induction Ketamine 1–2 mg/kg IV RSI induction; preferred in hypotension

docs.title: Allergic Reaction / Anaphylaxis
docs.text: Medication Dose Route Notes Epinephrine 1:1,000 0.3–0.5 mg IM (lateral thigh) First-line anaphylaxis Diphenhydramine 25–50 mg IV/IM Adjunct; not first-line Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV Prevent biphasic reaction Albuterol 2.5 mg Nebulized Bronchospasm

docs.title: Hypoglycemia
docs.text:
Medication Dose Route Notes Dextrose 50% 25 g (50 mL) IV BGL < 60 with AMS Glucagon 1 mg IM/IN No IV access Oral Glucose 15–30 g PO Alert patient; able to protect airway

Protocol Authority

All dosages listed are reference values. Always follow your current approved PEMS/locality protocols. When in doubt, consult medical direction.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: This administrative protocol outlines the procedure for safely transferring patients who do not require Advanced Life Support (ALS) care to Basic Life Support (BLS) providers. This ensures appropriate resource utilization while maintaining a high standard of patient care and safety during the transfer of the Attendant in Charge (AIC) role.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Complete and pertinent patient history
  • Evaluation of current complaint
  • Full set of baseline vital signs
  • Comprehensive physical assessment findings
  • ALS care is not currently medically necessary
  • ALS care will likely not be necessary in the near future (including pain management)
  • BLS provider capability and willingness to accept patient

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Comprehensive Assessment: Complete pertinent patient history, physical assessment, and full set of vital signs. • 2 Clinical Verification: Confirm ALS care is not currently medically necessary and will likely not be necessary in the near future (to include pain management). • 3 BLS Verification: Verify BLS provider is willing to accept responsibility and capable of providing a level of care that meets the assessed and documented needs. • 4 Transfer of Care: Transfer Attendant in Charge (AIC) responsibilities to the BLS provider. • • 5 Medical Control: Contact Medical Control when there is a concern of the appropriateness of the release. MC MC MC 6 BLS Documentation: Complete a Patient Care Report (PCR). 7 ALS Documentation: Complete documentation in, or as an addendum to, the PCR (physical findings, transfer of care, physician consultation). • 8 QA/QI Review: Any ALS Release to BLS must be reviewed by the agency Quality Assurance/Quality Improvement process. • • 9 Incident Reporting: Inappropriate release by QA/QI should be reported quarterly to the Poquoson Fire and Rescue PI Committee. •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Thorough Assessment: The ALS provider is strictly responsible for a comprehensive clinical assessment before handoff to ensure no hidden acuity exists.
  2. Documentation Requirements: Both providers have strict documentation responsibilities. The BLS provider completes the primary PCR, while the ALS provider must complete an addendum detailing physical findings, the transfer of care process, and any physician consultation.
  3. Medical Control: Always consult Medical Control (Poquoson OMD) for edge cases or when there is any concern about the appropriateness of the release to BLS.
  4. ⚠ Pain Management: Anticipated need for pain management is an ALS indicator; do not release patients to BLS if they will likely require analgesia in the near future.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: EMS providers act as mandated reporters under state law for suspected elder abuse, child abuse or neglect, and must report serious injuries or deaths resulting from hunting accidents.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Elder Criteria: Applies to adults 60+ or incapacitated adults 18+.
  • Child Criteria: Applies to minors.
  • Hunting Accidents: Serious bodily injury or death from hunting.
  • Psychological: Excessive passivity, fearful behavior, excessive aggression, crying, hyperactivity.
  • Physical: Injuries inconsistent with mechanism of injury (MOI).
  • Neglect: Inappropriate clothing for weather, inadequate hygiene, absence of caregivers, malnutrition.
  • Elder/Child Abuse Types: Physical, Sexual (or exploitation), Emotional/Psychological/Mental, Neglect, Abandonment.
  • Hunting Reporting: Must report within 5 days to Dept of Game and Inland Fisheries (DGIF).

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Assess and document psychological characteristics (passivity, fear, aggression, etc.). • • 2 Assess for physical signs or injuries that are inconsistent with the reported mechanism of injury (MOI). • • 3 Assess for signs of neglect (hygiene, clothing, malnutrition, absence of caregivers). • • 4 Report Elder Abuse: Notify local DSS or Adult Protective Services (APS). If imminent danger of death/serious injury or death occurred, report to medical examiner and law enforcement. • • 5 Report Child Abuse: Notify local DSS or Child Protective Services (CPS). If transporting, it is acceptable to report to the attending ED physician. • • 6 Report Hunting Injuries: Notify Dept of Game and Inland Fisheries (DGIF) for serious bodily injury or death. • • 7 Document all notifications (e.g., "made notification to Dr. [ ]", hotline calls) thoroughly on the PCR. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Mandated Reporter Legal Penalties: Failure to report child abuse leads to monetary penalties (§63.2-1509). Failure to report hunting accidents is a Class 4 misdemeanor.
  2. Hotlines & Contacts: APS 888-832-3858; DGIF 804-367-1258 or 804-367-2251.
  3. Documentation: Ensure all physician handoffs and hotline notifications are explicitly documented on the patient care report (PCR).

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Appropriately identify patients experiencing a mental health crisis that can benefit from direct transport to a behavioral health facility.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Suicidal Ideations / Homicidal Ideations
  • Seeking Substance abuse help
  • Mood disorder (Depression / Anxiety)
  • Psychosis / Schizophrenia / Bipolar
  • Is patient oriented to person, place and time?
  • Confirms prior psych history?
  • No concern for infectious process?
  • No indications of intentional overdose?

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Assess decision-making capacity • • 2 Direct Transport: Once identified, consider direct transport to a Mental Health Facility. • • 3 Medical Control: When in doubt regarding appropriate destination or patient status, contact Medical Control. • • 4 Psychiatric Emergencies: Acute psychiatric emergencies with significant risk of harm to self/others refusing treatment/transport: contact law enforcement regarding Emergency Custody Order (ECO) for psychiatric evaluation. • • 5 Medical Emergencies: Acute medical emergencies lacking decision-making capacity refusing treatment/transport: contact Medical Control regarding ECO for medical evaluation. MC must petition a magistrate. Contact law enforcement to assist. • • 6 Law Enforcement Custody: Patients in LE custody (including ECOs) or under arrest MUST have a law enforcement officer present at all times. • • 7 Restraints: If LE restraints utilized (handcuffs, flex-cuffs), LE officer SHOULD accompany the patient. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ Medical vs. Psychiatric ECO: A Psychiatric ECO is obtained by Law Enforcement for psychiatric evaluations. A Medical ECO requires Medical Control to petition a magistrate for patients lacking decision-making capacity due to medical emergencies.
  • ⚠ Patients under Law Enforcement custody or an ECO MUST have a law enforcement officer present at all times.
  • ⚠ If Law Enforcement restraints are used (e.g., handcuffs), the officer SHOULD accompany the patient in the ambulance.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Administrative - Communications
docs.text: This protocol outlines the procedures for effectively communicating with Medical Control and receiving facilities using the HEAR and COR systems. It establishes standardized reporting criteria for pre-arrival notifications and clear contingencies for communications failure or Mass-Casualty Incidents (MCI).

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • HEAR Criteria: BLS/ALS patients NOT requiring on-line physician intervention.
  • COR Criteria: Need for physician consultation, medication authorizations, or Stroke/Trauma/STEMI alerts.
  • Comm Failure: Unable to contact medical control after 4 attempts (2 via radio, 2 via cell phone).
  • MCI: Mass-casualty or disaster incidents.
  • Pre-arrival report needs
  • Unstable/unsecured airway needing closest hospital
  • Specialized team activation (Rape, Burn, STEMI)
  • Refusal consultation
  • ALS release to BLS authorization

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Use HEAR for notification onlyNotify hospitals of BLS/ALS patients NOT requiring on-line physician intervention. • • 2 Use COR for medical control/meds/alertsDirect contact with Medical Control for physician consultation. Used for Stroke, Trauma, STEMI alerts (providing Name/DOB). • • 3 Transmit standard pre-arrival reportInclude Unit ID/ETA, patient age/gender/chief complaint, history of present illness/injury, pertinent medical history, significant physical findings, vital signs, and treatment. • • 4 MCI comms suspension/supervisor relayRoutine ambulance-to-ED communications suspended. Transport group supervisor communicates directly to coordinating ED. • • 5 Comm Failure: act on standing ordersFollow appropriate Poquoson Patient Care Protocols based on clinical situation. All orders within scope of practice become standing orders. • • 6 Document comm failure/submit incident formDocument time, nature of comms problem, clinical status, and protocols used. Submit Medical Control Incident Form and PCR to Poquoson OMD within 48 hours. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Communication Failure Standing Orders: In the event of communications failure, act on standing orders within your scope of practice based on the clinical situation.
  2. ⚠ Medication Authorization: Medication and procedure authorizations must come directly from a Physician, PA, or NP.
  3. HEAR vs COR Distinction: Use the HEAR system for brief hospital notifications where no orders are needed. Use the COR system for direct physician consultation, alerts (Stroke, Trauma, STEMI), and medication authorizations.
  4. MCI Protocol Rules: During mass-casualty or disaster incidents, routine communications are suspended. The Transport group supervisor will communicate directly with the coordinating ED. Medical Control may grant a request to follow protocols as written.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Administrative - Death & Criteria for Withholding Resuscitation
docs.text: This protocol outlines the indications and legal requirements for withholding resuscitation efforts in the presence of death or valid Do Not Resuscitate (DNR) orders. It also establishes the clinical criteria and procedures for the termination of resuscitation (TOR) efforts when cardiac arrest is refractory to prehospital interventions.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Valid Virginia DDNR form (actual or copy/digital)
  • Valid DDNR from any State (actual or copy/digital)
  • VA approved DDNR ALERT bracelet/necklace
  • Decapitation or other obvious mortal injury
  • Rigor mortis
  • Dependent lividity
  • Decomposition
  • "Living will" or advanced directive only (without valid DDNR/POST)
  • POST, POLST, MOLST without specific DNR/DNI order
  • Any doubt regarding applicability or validity of DNR
  • Cardiac arrest is refractory to appropriate prehospital interventions
  • Arrest not witnessed by EMS
  • 20 minutes of continuous CPR
  • No return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC)
  • No shocks delivered

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Withhold CPR for obvious mortal injury, rigor mortis, dependent lividity, decomposition, or valid DDNR. • • 2 Initiate CPR if doubt exists regarding the DNR validity, or if only a living will/advanced directive is present. • • 3 Provide full BLS/ALS care prior to termination (BLS: AED, pulse ox, EtCO2, supraglottic airway; ALS: advanced airway, IV/IO, meds per ACLS). • • 4 Contact Medical Control for termination decision or DNR questions. Provide thorough report of history, condition, and treatment. • • 5 Remain on scene and notify law enforcement and Medical Examiner if terminated on scene. Support bystanders and family. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ Termination of resuscitation efforts is a Medical Control decision ONLY.
  • ⚠ Revocation of DNR: A DNR can be revoked at any time by the patient or authorized decision maker by physically destroying the DNR order form or oral expression of intent to revoke.
  • ⚠ 20 Minute TOR Criteria: Consider contacting Medical Control to request TOR if the arrest was not witnessed by EMS and after 20 minutes of continuous CPR: 1) There has been no return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) AND 2) No shocks were delivered.
  • • A DNR/DDNR/DNAR is applicable ONLY if the patient is in cardiac arrest.
  • • During mass-casualty incidents, resources must be directed toward viable patients.
  • • If resuscitation efforts are terminated at the scene, document time and physician name on the PCR. Be attentive to the emotional needs of any family members or bystanders present.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines and indications for utilizing Helicopter EMS for patient transport and specialty care.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • STEMI
  • Stroke
  • Trauma
  • Burns
  • Critical Pediatrics
  • Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD/BiVAD/VAD)
  • Wilderness rescue
  • Ambulance egress or access impeded at the scene by road conditions, weather, or traffic
  • The air ambulance service can provide needed medical capability at the scene

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Request HEMS: Request air ambulance standby/response according to department procedure (no medical control authorization needed). • • 2 Establish Landing Zone: Establish a safe and logistically feasible landing zone. • • 3 Provide Patient Info: Establish number of patients, primary injuries/medical condition, and approximate patient weight(s). Forward this information to the responding air medical service. • • 4 Prepare Patient: Trauma patients should be fully immobilized. IV/IO access should be established as time allows (within provider scope). • • 5 Designate Reporter: On scene, designate one individual who will be responsible for providing the patient report(s) to the flight crew upon arrival. • • 6 Provide Documentation: Ensure PCR is provided to receiving facility as per 12 VAC 5-31. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ VAD patients require coordination with VAD Coordinator or Medical Control.
  • HEMS requested via department procedure without online medical control authorization.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Procedures for receiving a surrendered infant under the Virginia Safe Haven Law (Reference §18.2-371, §18.2-371.1, §40.1-103). Providers in the Poquoson Fire and Rescue region will follow all laws regarding the safe surrender of infants.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Must be within the first 30 days of the child’s life
  • Child must have been delivered in a manner reasonably calculated to ensure the child’s safety
  • Surrendered to a hospital that provides 24-hour emergency services or to an attended rescue squad
  • Child Protective Services
  • Local Law Enforcement

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Receive infant if safely surrendered to attended rescue squad • • 2 Assess infant and provide medical care per Poquoson Protocols • • 3 Contact Child Protective Services or local Law Enforcement • • 4 Transport to appropriate facility if indicated • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ Infant must be within first 30 days of life.
  • ⚠ Virginia Safe Haven Law grants immunity from civil or criminal liability to receiving providers.
  • Ensure safe and supportive environment for the surrendering parent if present.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Administrative - Patient Refusal of Treatment-Transport
docs.text: Guidelines for assessing capacity, identifying surrogate decision makers, and obtaining valid patient refusals or Emergency Custody Orders (ECOs) for patients declining otherwise indicated care or transport.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Patient must be at least 14 years of age
  • Not subject to a court decree of incapacity or incompetence
  • Fully oriented (person, place, time, events)
  • Does not pose a foreseeable risk of harm to self or others
  • Understands current clinical situation and proposed plan for treatment/transport
  • Understands potential risks and benefits of proposed plan and alternatives
  • Not under undue external influence (consider private discussion)
  • No condition present which may impair decision-making capacity

In descending order of authority: - Legal agent, guardian, or committee (appointed by a court of law) - Medical power of attorney (appointed by the patient) - Patient’s spouse – except in cases where divorce has been filed - Adult child of the patient - Parent of the patient - Adult sibling of the patient - Other adult relative of the patient in descending order of blood relationship

  • Psychiatric ECO: Acute psychiatric emergencies who present a significant risk of harm to themselves or others and are refusing treatment or transport.
  • Medical ECO: Acute medical emergencies who lack decision-making capacity or competence and are refusing treatment or transport.

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Conduct a thorough assessment of the patient including vital signs and physical exam in accordance with relevant patient care protocols. • • 2 Assess right/capacity to refuse (≥ 14 years of age, fully oriented, understands risks/benefits/alternatives, no risk to self/others). • • 3 Identify appropriate surrogate decision maker (in descending order) if the patient lacks capacity or competence. • • 4 Clearly document information provided; obtain signatures for refusal from patient or surrogate and at least 1 adult witness. • • 5 Contact law enforcement regarding issuance of a Psychiatric ECO for patients with acute psychiatric emergencies risk of harm. • • 6 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control regarding issuance of a Medical ECO for acute medical emergencies lacking capacity. MC MC MC MC MC 7 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for assistance if unsure of capacity, surrogate validity, or if patient refuses despite severe risk. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ Medical ECOs: For medical emergencies, issuance of an emergency custody order (ECO) for medical evaluation requires a licensed physician to petition a magistrate or special justice. Early discussion with Poquoson OMD / Medical Control to provide a detailed description of the current clinical situation, capacity assessment, and concerns is critical. • ⚠ Medical Power of Attorney: Only comes into effect when a patient lacks decision-making capacity. Attempt to obtain a copy of any documentation assigning guardianship or medical POA. If unavailable, accept assertion in good faith absent contradictory evidence. • ⚠ Minor Exceptions: A minor may be deemed an adult for the purpose of giving consent to surgical and medical treatment for her child. • ⚠ Emergency Consent for Minors: Treatment and/or transport of a minor is authorized if delay may adversely affect the minor’s recovery and no authorized person is available to provide consent. If the minor is 14 years of age or older and physically capable of giving consent, such consent shall be obtained first.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines and procedures for physical and chemical patient restraint to ensure the safety of patients and responders. Patient restraint should be utilized only if the patient is exhibiting behavior that is an immediate danger to self or others.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Patient is exhibiting behavior that is an immediate danger to self or others
  • Determine minimum level of physical restraint required
  • Evaluate personnel needed to safely restrain patient
  • Hypoxia
  • Hypoglycemia
  • Head injury
  • Signs of drug or alcohol use
  • Unsteady gait, slurred speech
  • Other signs of potential mental incapacity
  • Manner of violence (record comments verbatim)
  • Why provider felt threatened
  • Concern for outcome without interventions
  • Could patient be treated without restraints?
  • Law enforcement present/accompanied patient to ED
  • Kind and location of restraints used
  • Frequency of respiratory/mental status change assessments

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Request Law Enforcement if neededCall for assistance prior to attempting restraint procedures. • • 2 Rule out/treat medical causesConsider and treat medical causes of combativeness (hypoxia, head injury, hypoglycemia). • • 3 Place patient supine on backboardNEVER PRONE. • • 4 Secure extremities/check circulation every 15 minsAttempt lower extremities first (soft restraints around both ankles), then arms at sides. Secure backboard to stretcher. If necessary, utilize cervical spine precautions. • • 5 Apply SpO2 and monitor respiratory statusEvaluate respiratory/cardiac status to assure no airway compromise exists; apply supplemental oxygen if indicated. • • 6 Avoid impeding respiratory function with chest strapsDO NOT tighten chest straps to the point of impeding respiratory function. • • 7 Chemical Restraint: Assess vitals <5 mins then q10 minsAnd prior to any repeat dose. Monitor ECG, obtain 12-Lead, consider IN medications and establish IV/IO if possible. • 8 Refer to Behavioral Emergencies Protocol for sedativesSedative agents may be needed to restrain violently combative patients. (Refer to Poquoson Protocol(s) for Behavioral Emergencies). •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ NEVER restrain a patient prone.
  • ⚠ Restraint should be minimum level necessary to accomplish patient care and ensure safe transportation (soft restraints may be sufficient).
  • ⚠ Ensure restraints do not impede evaluation or respiratory function.
  • Avoid placing restraints in such a way as to prevent evaluation of the patient’s medical needs.
  • Constant evaluation of your patient’s airway status is extremely important.
  • Do not endanger yourself, other responders, or the patient.
  • Document reason for lack of vital signs assessment if unable to obtain them.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Administrative - STEMI Field Triage
docs.text: This administrative protocol establishes the operational guidelines to rapidly identify patients experiencing an acute myocardial infarction and ensure safe, expedited transport to definitive care. Timely acquisition of a 12-lead ECG, appropriate routing, and early facility notification are critical to reducing time to intervention.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Cardiac symptoms
  • High clinical suspicion of Acute MI
  • Machine interpretation reads "ACUTE MI" or "STEMI"
  • 1mm+ ST elevation in 2+ contiguous leads
  • Compromised airway
  • Impending circulatory collapse
  • Atypical or indeterminate ECGs

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Acquire 12-Lead ECGMust be completed within 5 minutes of patient arrival. • • 2 ECG InterpretationIdentify STEMI based on cardiac symptoms AND 1mm+ ST elevation in 2+ contiguous leads. • 3 Routing - PCI Capable HospitalTransport to the closest PCI capable facility if EMS ground transport time is < 30 minutes. • • 4 Routing - HEMS ConsiderationIf ground transport to a PCI facility is > 30 minutes, evaluate Helicopter EMS (HEMS) transport within 30 minutes. • • 5 Routing - Closest EDTransport to the closest ED (regardless of PCI capability) if the patient has a compromised airway or impending circulatory collapse. • • 6 NotificationsActivate STEMI alert system to closest PCI center within 30 minutes. Call COR with "Pre-Hospital STEMI Alert" and patient Name/DOB. Transmit ECG and provide printed/electronic copy to facility/HEMS. • • 7 Serial ECGsFor atypical/indeterminate ECGs with high suspicion, transmit ECG, contact Medical Control, and obtain serial ECGs every 10 minutes. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Do NOT provide patient identifiers (Name/DOB) over the HEAR radio system. Call COR with "Pre-Hospital STEMI Alert" and identifiers.
  2. Time targets are critical: Acquire 12-lead within 5 minutes of arrival, and activate the STEMI alert system to the closest PCI center within 30 minutes.
  3. A printed or electronic copy of the ECG containing the patient's Name, DOB, and Time must be provided to the receiving facility or HEMS crew.
  4. When in doubt regarding ECG interpretation or transport routing, contact Medical Control.
  5. ⚠ Unstable patients (compromised airway or impending circulatory collapse) must be transported to the closest Emergency Department, regardless of PCI capability.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Administrative - Safe Transport of Pediatric Patients
Overview
docs.text: Best practices for the ambulance transportation of ill or injured pediatric patients to provide safe transport for all occupants. Ambulances are NOT EXEMPT from state child safety laws.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Pediatric patient requiring transport
  • Need for medical or trauma transport
  • Patient weight < 40 kg (88 lbs) requires child restraint
  • Patient status: Trauma vs Medical
  • Passenger considerations (parents, caregivers, non-patients)
  • State child safety law compliance

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Secure Equipment: Secure all monitoring devices and other equipment to prevent injury. • • 2 Safe Driving: Drive cautiously and at safe speeds, observing applicable traffic laws. • • 3 Child Restraint: Ensure pediatric patients < 40 kg (88 lbs) are restrained with approved child restraint devices secured per the manufacturer’s instructions. • • 4 Provider Safety: Ensure that all EMS providers use available restraining devices or systems during transport. • • 5 Caregiver Safety: Secure parents or caregivers using safety belts. • • 6 Alternative Transport: Attempt to arrange for transportation of adults and children who are not patients by alternative means. • • 7 Comfort Measures: For pediatric patients having medical conditions aggravated by stress, make every reasonable attempt to maximize safety while comforting the child. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ NEVER hold, attempt to hold, or permit parents or caregivers to hold the pediatric patient during transport. • ⚠ Ambulances are NOT EXEMPT from state child safety laws. DO NOT allow parents, caregivers or any passenger to be unrestrained during transport. • ⚠ Infants and children meeting Trauma Triage criteria should be transported in a child restraint device unless requiring relocation to or placement on an immobilization device.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: To rapidly evaluate and get patients experiencing a suspected stroke to the most appropriate definitive care facility based on symptom onset time and severity.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Time of onset / "Last seen normal" time
  • Symptom onset < 24 hours vs > 24 hours (or unknown)
  • BEFAST Exam: Altered mental status, abnormal/slurred speech, facial droop, unilateral weakness, balance instability, vision changes
  • RACE Exam: Facial palsy/droop, arm weakness, leg weakness
  • Blood Glucose: Must be > 60 mg/dL (if < 60, refer to Hypoglycemia protocol)
  • LVO Suspicion: RACE Score 5-9 indicates suspected Large Vessel Occlusion
  • Immediate Life-Threats (e.g., uncontrolled airway, cardiac arrest)

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Time Management: Maintain a goal scene time of less than 15 minutes. • • 2 Diagnostics: Assess blood glucose. If < 60 mg/dL, treat per Hypoglycemia protocol. • • 3 Neurological Assessment: Evaluate patient using BEFAST criteria and RACE scale. • • 4 Immediate Life-Threats: If uncontrolled airway, cardiac arrest, etc. cannot be addressed during transport, route to the nearest ED. • • 5 Routing (< 24 Hours & LVO): If Symptoms < 24 Hours (or unknown) AND Suspected LVO (RACE Score 5-9), transport to closest Comprehensive Stroke Center IF it does not increase transport time by 30 mins or greater. If it does, transport to nearest Primary Stroke Center. • • 6 Routing (> 24 Hours OR No LVO): If Symptoms > 24 Hours OR Low Suspicion for LVO (RACE Score 0-4), transport to closest Stroke Center (Acute Stroke Ready, Primary, or Comprehensive). • • 7 Stroke Alert Activation: If RACE ≥ 1, activate stroke alert system by notifying appropriate facility early. • • 8 Communication: Early notification to Medical Control and/or Stroke Center. During transport, call COR with Name and DOB for rapid registration. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Time is Brain: It is critical to accurately record the "last seen normal" time to determine the appropriate treatment window.
  2. ⚠ Information Gathering: Gather family contact info and provide it to the receiving facility for immediate consent and history needs.
  3. • Routing Exceptions: Immediate life-threats that cannot be managed during transport supersede stroke routing guidelines; go directly to the nearest ED.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Administrative - Transportation and Destination Determination
docs.text: Respecting patient decision-making capacity while ensuring safe, appropriate medical transportation. This protocol balances patient hospital preference with the need to route time-critical conditions to specialized facilities.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Patient decision-making capacity
  • Hospital preference stated by patient
  • Time of symptom onset
  • STEMI criteria met
  • Stroke/CVA criteria met
  • Other life-threatening time-critical conditions
  • Patient Preference vs. Closest Facility
  • Time-Critical Exceptions
  • HEMS availability and ETA

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Transport to patient choice: Transport capable patients to the ED of their choice (if no exceptions) • • 2 Reroute for STEMI criteria: Transport to STEMI Receiving Center per Poquoson Regional STEMI Plan • • 3 Reroute for Stroke criteria: Transport to designated stroke center per Poquoson Stroke Triage Plan • • 4 Transport to closest: Transport to the closest most appropriate hospital if no patient preference • • 5 Document reason: Document reason for any reroute or destination change on the PCR • • 6 Contact HEMS: Contact HEMS without Medical Control if beneficial to the patient • • 7 Initiate ground transport: Initiate ground transport to closest hospital/helispot if HEMS > 10 min delay • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Destination Changes: Any time a patient is transported to the most appropriate facility other than the one requested, the reason for the change and destination shall be documented on the Patient Care Report (PCR).
  2. ⚠ Time-Critical Exceptions: STEMI, Stroke/CVA, and other life-threatening conditions the AIC believes to be time critical override patient hospital preference to ensure specialized care.
  3. ⚠ HEMS Delays: If the patient is ready for transport AND air ambulance is delayed >10 minutes, initiate ground transport to the closest hospital or helispot/helipad.
  4. Alternate Destinations: If approved by the Poquoson OMD / Emergency Physician, alternate destinations may be established.
  5. Internal Policy: Agencies shall have an internal policy explaining destination policy specific to that agency (i.e., time away from community limits).

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: This protocol establishes the criteria and routing instructions to rapidly triage and transport trauma victims to definitive care. It outlines physiologic, mechanism of injury, and special considerations to ensure optimal patient outcomes through timely arrival at an appropriate trauma center.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations

Mechanism of Injury Criteria: - Ejection (partial/complete) from automobile - Death in same passenger compartment - Auto vs pedestrian/bicyclist (thrown, run over, >20 mph) - Motorcycle crash >20 mph

Physiologic Criteria: - GCS < 14 - Systolic BP < 90 mmHg Immediate Life-Threats: - Unmanageable airway - Uncontrolled hemorrhage - Traumatic cardiac arrest

Special Considerations: - Age: >55 years old - End-Stage Renal Disease (Dialysis) - Time-Sensitive Extremities: Open fractures, neurovascular compromise - Burns: Absent other trauma, burns meeting criteria (full/partial >10% TBSA, circumferential, electrical/chemical, inhalation, face/hands/feet/genitals) go to Burn Center. - Pregnancy: >20 weeks pregnant → trauma center or OB-capable hospital. - Pediatrics: Severely injured go to pediatric trauma center (CHKD or VCU).

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Scene Time Management: Maintain a goal scene time of < 10 minutes. • • 2 Immediate Life-Threats Routing: If unmanageable airway, uncontrolled hemorrhage, or traumatic cardiac arrest, transport immediately to closest ED. • • 3 Physiologic Criteria Met Routing: Transport to closest appropriate trauma center (Level I or II) within 30-min ground transport. • • 4 Out of Range (Ground) / HEMS Consideration: If not within 30-min ground transport, transport to closest hospital UNLESS Helicopter EMS (HEMS) can deliver to trauma center faster. • • 5 Mechanism-Only Routing: If patient meets mechanism criteria but NOT physiologic/anatomic criteria, contact Medical Control to determine destination. MC MC MC MC MC 6 Burn Center Routing: For burns meeting criteria absent other trauma, transport directly to Burn Center. • • 7 Pediatric Trauma Routing: Transport severely injured pediatric patients to a pediatric trauma center (CHKD or VCU). • • 8 Trauma Alerts: Establish early radio contact with the receiving trauma center. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ Provider Judgment: Providers have the expertise to make clinical judgments on atypical presentations and route accordingly.
  • ⚠ Anticoagulants / Bleeding Disorders: Patients on anticoagulants or with bleeding disorders are at high risk for deterioration; contact Medical Control and consider trauma center routing.
  • ⚠ Pregnancy: Patients >20 weeks pregnant require specialized assessment; route to a trauma center or an OB-capable hospital.
  • ⚠ Contacting Medical Control: Contact Medical Control whenever destination determination is unclear, especially for mechanism-only criteria or high-risk comorbidities.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
General - Asystole and Pulseless Electrical Activity
docs.text: Resuscitation guidelines for Asystole and Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA), emphasizing high-quality CPR and identifying reversible causes.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Any patient without a palpable pulse or respiration.
  • Asystole or Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA) evidence on the cardiac monitor (check two leads).
  • AED advising not to shock.
  • Patients with a valid durable DNR, rigor mortis, lividity, decomposition, or injuries inconsistent with survival are excluded.

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol. Determine DNR status. Refer to Death and Criteria for Withholding Resuscitation protocol. Consider spinal motion restriction. • • 2 Begin chest compressions. Refer to Adult / Pediatric Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) procedure. Children/Infants ONLY: If unconscious and hypotensive, with HR less than 60, begin chest compressions. Alternate compressors every 2 minutes or sooner if fatigued. Consider mechanical CPR device, if available. • • 3 Identify and treat potentially reversible causes. See Pearls below. • • 4 Ensure adequate oxygenation. Refer to Airway Management and Ventilatory Support procedure. • • 5 Obtain and transmit a 12-Lead ECG. • • 6 Obtain IV/IO access. • 7 Monitor ETCO2. If ETCO2 < 10 or dropping, reassess and optimize compression rate, depth, and recoil. • • 8 Apply AED / Assess cardiac rhythm every 2 minutes. If AED is advising to shock or rhythm is VF or VT (as determined by EMT-I or P), defibrillate at maximum joules (or 2 J/kg for pediatrics), then refer to Cardiac Arrest: Ventricular Fibrillation / Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia protocol. If AED is advising no shock or rhythm is asystole or PEA (as determined by EMT-I or P), continue CPR then refer to Cardiac Arrest: Asystole / Pulseless Electrical Activity protocol. • • 9 Children/Infants ONLY: If unconscious and hypotensive, with HR less than 60, administer:PEDIATRIC: Epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg IV/IO [0.1 mg/mL] every 3 – 5 minutes, as needed. • 10 For cardiac arrest in renal dialysis patients, patients with crush injuries, suspected calcium channel blocker overdose, or suspected hyperkalemia only, also administer:ADULT: Calcium Chloride 1 g IV/IO slow push followed by 20 mL 0.9% sodium chloride flush.Sodium Bicarbonate 50 mEq IV/IO slow push.Repeat in 5 minutes if no change. • 11 For cardiac arrest in renal dialysis patients, patients with crush injuries, suspected calcium channel blocker overdose, or suspected hyperkalemia only, also administer:PEDIATRIC: Calcium Chloride 20 mg/kg (max dose 1 g) IV/IO slow push followed by 20 mL 0.9% sodium chloride flush.Sodium Bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg (max dose 50 mEq) IV/IO slow push.Repeat in 5 minutes if no change. • 12 If ROSC is achieved, refer to Post Cardiac Arrest Care (ROSC) protocol.If ROSC is not achieved, refer to Death and Criteria for Withholding Resuscitation protocol. Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for guidance as needed. Note: Once initiated, termination of resuscitative efforts is a Poquoson OMD / Medical Control decision only. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ Push hard and fast: rate 100 – 120 bpm. Allow complete chest recoil. • ⚠ Minimize interruptions in chest compressions. • ⚠ Termination of resuscitative efforts is a Poquoson OMD / Medical Control decision only.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Cardiac - Symptomatic Bradycardia
docs.text: OVERVIEW: Symptomatic bradycardia occurs when the heart rate is less than 60 beats per minute with signs of inadequate perfusion despite adequate oxygenation and ventilation. This protocol addresses unstable patients requiring immediate intervention to restore adequate cardiac output and tissue perfusion.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Sustained ventricular rate < 60 bpm
  • Age-based assessment
  • Onset and duration of symptoms
  • Recent medications
  • History of cardiac disease
  • Hypotension (age based)
  • Signs of shock
  • Altered mental status
  • Pulmonary edema
  • Dyspnea
  • Chest pain
  • Asymptomatic/stable bradycardia (excluded - monitor closely)
  • Neonates < 28 days (see Neonatal Resuscitation protocol)
  • Medication-induced bradycardia
  • AV blocks
  • Sinus node dysfunction

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol • • 2 Assess and continuously monitor cardiac rhythm • 3 Atropine sulfateADULT: 1 mg IV/IOPEDIATRIC: 0.02 mg/kg IV/IOMinimum dose: 0.1 mg, Maximum dose: 0.5 mgMay repeat once after 5 minutes, if needed 4 If hypotensive or in shock, initiate transcutaneous pacing. Follow Transcutaneous Pacing procedure • 5 Maintain mean arterial pressure (MAP) above 65 mmHg. If pacing is ineffective, administer:Epinephrine infusion 2 – 10 mcg/min IV/IO(Add 1 mg of Epinephrine [1mg/mL] to 100 mL of 0.9% Sodium chloride = 10 mcg/mL) 6 Maintain age adjusted SBP. If pacing is ineffective, administer:PEDIATRIC: Epinephrine 0.1 - 1 mcg/kg/min IV/IO infusionMax dose: 10 mcg/minUnder 1 year old: Titrate to SBP > 70 mmHgOver 1 year old: Titrate to SBP > 90 mmHg 7 To maintain MAP > 65 until a vasopressor infusion is prepared, consider:ADULT: Push dose Epinephrine 5-10 mcg IV/IO every 2-3 minutes(Add 100 mcg Epinephrine (1mL of 0.1 mg/mL) to 9mL of 0.9% Normal Saline = 10 mcg/mL) 8 Contact Medical Control for guidance as neededConsider contacting Medical Control at a pediatric specialty center (VCU CHoR or CHKD) for children and infants with symptomatic bradycardia MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. Close monitoring during fluid administration including frequent reassessment of respiratory effort, lung sounds, and edema.
  2. ⚠ Asymptomatic/stable bradycardia patients are excluded from this protocol - monitor closely for any change in status.
  3. ⚠ For neonates (less than 28 days of age), refer to Neonatal Resuscitation protocol.
  4. Atropine may be ineffective in high-degree AV blocks and transcutaneous pacing should be considered early in these cases.
  5. Ensure adequate oxygenation and ventilation prior to medication administration - many cases of bradycardia in children are due to hypoxia.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Cardiac arrest is the cessation of cardiac mechanical activity resulting in the absence of circulation. This protocol addresses the two primary categories of cardiac arrest: Ventricular Fibrillation (V-Fib) / Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (V-Tach) which are shockable rhythms, and Asystole / Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA) which are non-shockable rhythms. Immediate high-quality CPR and rapid defibrillation (for shockable rhythms) are critical for survival.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Witnessed or unwitnessed collapse
  • Estimated downtime
  • Bystander CPR performed
  • Recent illness or trauma history
  • Medication history (especially cardiac meds)
  • Dialysis status
  • Unresponsive
  • Absent or abnormal breathing (agonal respirations)
  • No pulse palpable (carotid or femoral)
  • V-Fib/V-Tach or Asystole/PEA on monitor
  • Cyanosis or pallor

H's and T's - Potentially Reversible Causes: - Hypoxia - Hyperkalemia or Hypokalemia - Hyperglycemia or Hypoglycemia - Hypovolemia - Hydrogen Ion (acidosis) - Tension pneumothorax/hemothorax - Tamponade (Cardiac) - Thrombosis (Cardiac/pulmonary) - Toxins - Trauma - Tablets (overdose)

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Scene Safety / Initial Contact:• Ensure scene safety and appropriate PPE.• Determine DNR status (refer to Death and Criteria for Withholding Resuscitation protocol).• Patients with valid durable DNR or injuries inconsistent with survival are excluded. • • 2 Begin High-Quality CPR:• Push hard and fast at rate 100 – 120 bpm.• Allow complete chest recoil.• Minimize interruptions in chest compressions.• Change compressors every 2 minutes or sooner if fatigued. • • 3 Airway Management:• Apply BVM with high-flow O₂.• Use quantitative EtCO₂ monitoring with BVM:  - If EtCO₂ < 10 mmHg, improve compressions  - If sudden increase above 30-40 mmHg, check for ROSC• Give continuous compressions with advanced airway. • • 4 AED / Rhythm Assessment:• Apply AED and assess cardiac rhythm every 2 minutes.• Identify rhythm: V-Fib/V-Tach (shockable) or Asystole/PEA (non-shockable). • • 5 Defibrillation - V-Fib/V-Tach Only:• ADULT: 200 joules Biphasic / 360 joules Monophasic, or manufacturer's recommendation• PEDIATRIC: 2 joules/kg initial, 4 joules/kg subsequent• After each defibrillation, perform 2 minutes of CPR, then reassess rhythm.• Ensure optimal pad placement; consider adjustment if multiple shocks delivered without rhythm change. • • 6 Spinal Motion Restriction:• Consider spinal motion restriction if indicated. • • 7 IV/IO Access:• Obtain IV/IO access (humeral IO preferred if IV unavailable).• Administer fluids and medications. • 8 Epinephrine - All Rhythms:• ADULT: 1 mg IV/IO [0.1 mg/mL concentration]• PEDIATRIC: 0.01 mg/kg IV/IO [0.1 mg/mL concentration]• Frequency: Repeat every 3–5 minutes during cardiac arrest• Administer without interruption of CPR. • 9 Amiodarone - V-Fib/V-Tach Only (if persists after defibrillation and epinephrine):• ADULT: 300 mg IV/IO slow push• PEDIATRIC: 5 mg/kg IV/IO slow push. Maximum dose 300 mg.• Alternative if Amiodarone unavailable: Contact Medical Control for guidance • MC 10 Fluid Administration - Asystole/PEA Only:• ADULT: 0.9% Sodium Chloride up to 1 L in 250 – 500 mL boluses• PEDIATRIC: 0.9% Sodium Chloride 20 mL/kg• Repeat as needed to maintain adequate perfusion, up to 60 mL/kg total. • 11 Calcium Chloride and Sodium Bicarbonate - Special Circumstances Only:(For cardiac arrest in renal dialysis patients, crush injuries, suspected calcium channel blocker overdose, or suspected hyperkalemia)• ADULT:  - Calcium Chloride 1 g IV/IO slow push, followed by 20 mL 0.9% sodium chloride flush  - Sodium Bicarbonate 50 mEq IV/IO slow push• PEDIATRIC:  - Calcium Chloride 20 mg/kg (max dose 1g) IV/IO slow push, followed by 20 mL 0.9% sodium chloride flush  - Sodium Bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg (max dose 50 mEq) IV/IO slow push• May repeat in 5 minutes if no change. • MC 12 Identify and Treat Reversible Causes:• Continuously assess for H's and T's throughout resuscitation.• Treat underlying cause if identified. • • 13 Termination of Resuscitation (TOR):Contact Medical Control to request TOR if ALL criteria met:• Arrest was not witnessed by EMS• After 20 minutes of continuous CPR with no ROSC• AND no shocks were delivered • MC 14 Post-Resuscitation:• If ROSC achieved, refer to Post Cardiac Arrest Care (ROSC) protocol.• If ROSC not achieved, refer to Death and Criteria for Withholding Resuscitation protocol. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ High-Quality CPR is Essential: Push hard and fast at rate 100 – 120 bpm. Allow complete chest recoil. Minimize interruptions. Change compressors every 2 minutes or sooner if fatigued. Give continuous compressions with advanced airway.

  2. ⚠ EtCO₂ Monitoring: Use quantitative EtCO₂ monitoring with BVM. If EtCO₂ < 10 mmHg, improve compressions. If sudden increase above 30-40 mmHg, check for ROSC.

  3. ⚠ Medication Administration: Give all medications without interruption of CPR. Epinephrine should be administered every 3–5 minutes throughout the arrest.

  4. ⚠ Defibrillation Technique: Ensure optimal pad placement. Consider adjustment of pad position if multiple shocks delivered without rhythm change.

  5. ⚠ DNR Status: Patients with a valid durable DNR or injuries inconsistent with survival are excluded from this protocol. Determine DNR status early.

  6. Shockable vs. Non-Shockable Rhythms:

  7. Shockable: V-Fib and Pulseless V-Tach require immediate defibrillation followed by 2 minutes of CPR.

  8. Non-Shockable: Asystole and PEA require continuous CPR with epinephrine administration; defibrillation is NOT indicated unless rhythm converts to V-Fib/V-Tach.

  9. Special Circumstances for Calcium Chloride and Sodium Bicarbonate: These medications are reserved for specific clinical scenarios only: renal dialysis patients, crush injuries, suspected calcium channel blocker overdose, or suspected hyperkalemia.

  10. Pediatric Considerations: Maximum doses apply to pediatric patients: Amiodarone 300 mg, Calcium Chloride 1 g, Sodium Bicarbonate 50 mEq. Use pediatric-specific dosing for all medications.

  11. Termination of Resuscitation Criteria: All three criteria must be met before requesting TOR from Medical Control: unwitnessed arrest by EMS, >20 minutes of CPR without ROSC, and no shocks delivered.

  12. Continuous Assessment: Throughout resuscitation, continuously assess for and treat potentially reversible causes (H's and T's). This may be the key to achieving ROSC.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Resuscitation guidelines for traumatic cardiac arrest, prioritizing hemorrhage control, reversible causes, and rapid transport over prolonged scene CPR.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Blunt or penetrating trauma
  • Mechanism of injury indicating severe trauma
  • Pulseless
  • Apnea or agonal respirations
  • Medical incident vs. Trauma
  • Obvious signs of death (excluded from protocol)
  • Withholding CPR for Asystole on EMS arrival

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Control major active external bleeding / Begin CPR • • 2 Open the airway / Ventilate • • 3 Apply AED / Monitor (Assess rhythm before CPR if possible) • • 4 Asystole on arrival: Consider withholding CPR • • 5 PEA / VF / VT: Continue CPR / Defibrillate if indicated • • 6 Obtain IV/IO access (x2 preferred) • 7 If blood unavailable: 0.9% Sodium Chloride up to 1 L IV/IO (Adult) / 20 mL/kg IV/IO (Ped) • 8 Assess Shock Index > 1 / Resuscitate prior to intubation • 9 Perform bilateral chest decompression for tension pneumo • 10 Rapid transport to Level I/II Trauma Center (Goal < 10 mins) • • 11 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for guidance / destination MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ Asystole on arrival survival <1%: Consider withholding resuscitation if the rhythm is asystole on EMS arrival, as survival rates are extremely low unless loss of pulses was witnessed by EMS with immediate intervention. • ⚠ Prioritize reversible causes over prolonged CPR: Efficacy of CPR in trauma is unclear. Focus on immediate reversible causes such as chest decompression, volume expansion, and airway management. CPR is needed to circulate any fluids/medications. • ⚠ Do not delay transport for establishing IVs/ALS. • ⚠ Shock Index = Heart Rate / Systolic Blood Pressure. Higher shock indices correlate with an increased risk of post-intubation hypotension and cardiac arrest. • If available, blood products should be used rather than 0.9% Sodium Chloride for the treatment of hemorrhagic shock. • Assess the scene and patient closely. If the degree of sustained trauma appears disproportionately minor, consider the possibility that a medical incident may have preceded or been induced by the injury.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Cardiac - Chest Pain / ACS
docs.text: OVERVIEW: Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) encompasses a spectrum of conditions resulting from reduced blood flow to the heart muscle, including unstable angina, non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), and ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). Prompt recognition and intervention are critical to reduce myocardial damage and improve patient outcomes. This protocol addresses both typical and atypical presentations of cardiac ischemia.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Chest pain or discomfort (pressure, squeezing, fullness)
  • Duration and onset of symptoms
  • Radiation of pain to arm, jaw, neck, back
  • Previous cardiac history
  • Risk factors: hypertension, diabetes, smoking, family history
  • Chest pain/discomfort
  • Dyspnea
  • Diaphoresis
  • Nausea/vomiting
  • Syncope or near-syncope
  • Palpitations
  • Anxiety, sense of impending doom
  • Acute Myocardial Infarction (STEMI/NSTEMI)
  • Unstable Angina
  • Aortic Dissection
  • Pulmonary Embolism
  • Pneumothorax
  • Pericarditis/Myocarditis
  • GERD/Esophageal spasm
  • Musculoskeletal pain

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Scene Safety / Initial Assessment:• Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact Poquoson Protocol.• If stroke suspected, refer to Stroke / TIA Poquoson Protocol. • • 2 Airway / Respiratory Management:• If respiratory distress, refer to Airway Management and Ventilatory Support procedure and Respiratory Distress Poquoson Protocol.• Assess SpO2. • • 3 Glucose Assessment:• Check blood glucose level.• Refer to Hyperglycemia / Hypoglycemia Poquoson Protocol if indicated. • • 4 12-Lead ECG Acquisition (EMT):• Consider assessing EtCO2 and/or SpCO as indicated.• Acquire 12-Lead ECG within 5 minutes of patient contact.• Transmit to receiving facility within 10 minutes of patient contact. • • 5 12-Lead ECG Interpretation - STEMI Criteria:• Intermediate and Paramedic providers may interpret as STEMI if:  - Symptoms consistent with ACS/myocardial ischemia AND  - ≥ 1 mm ST segment elevation in two or more contiguous leads • 6 STEMI Alert / Notification:• Contact receiving facility via COR and provide "Pre-Hospital STEMI Alert" with patient's name and date of birth to facilitate rapid registration.• Do NOT provide patient identifiers over the HEAR.• Consider destination per Administrative Policy - STEMI Field Triage if indicated. • 7 Extended ECG Assessment:• If 12-Lead ECG is indeterminate or high suspicion for cardiac ischemia, obtain serial ECGs at 10-minute intervals.• Contact Poquoson OMD for guidance. MC MC 8 IV/IO Access:• Establish IV / IO access when indicated (refer to Intraosseous Access procedure). • 9 Cardiac Monitoring:• Consider continuous cardiac monitoring. • 10 Aspirin Administration (for ACS/STEMI):• Aspirin (NSAID - inhibits platelet action preventing clot formation)• Indication: Acute coronary syndromes (ACS)/STEMI• Confirm all "Rights" (Right patient, medication, dose, route, time)• Ensure medications not expired, assess for drug-drug interactions• Contraindications: Allergy to Aspirin/NSAIDs• Precautions: History of GI bleeding/peptic ulcers, chronic kidney disease, trauma • 11 Nitroglycerin Administration:• Administer Nitroglycerin per Pain Control Poquoson Protocol for chest pain.• Confirm all "Rights" prior to administration. • 12 Analgesia:• Consider analgesia (refer to Pain Control Poquoson Protocol). • 13 Medical Control Contact:• Contact Poquoson OMD for guidance as needed. MC MC MC 14 Right-Sided / 15-Lead ECG:• Consider right-sided 12-Lead or 15-Lead ECG for additional assessment. MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. Atypical Presentations: ACS may present as abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, syncope, dyspnea, back pain, neck/jaw pain, or arm pain/discomfort without classic chest pain. Maintain high index of suspicion in at-risk populations.

  2. Aspirin Administration:

  3. Class: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID)
  4. Action: Inhibits platelet action preventing clot formation
  5. Indication: Acute coronary syndromes (ACS)/STEMI
  6. Contraindications: Allergy to Aspirin/NSAIDs
  7. Precautions: History of GI bleeding/peptic ulcers, chronic kidney disease, trauma

  8. Side Effects: Indigestion

  9. ⚠ Medication Safety: Confirm all "Rights" (Right patient, medication, dose, route, time), ensure medications not expired, assess for drug-drug interactions before administration.

  10. ⚠ STEMI Time-Critical: Acquire 12-Lead ECG within 5 minutes and transmit within 10 minutes. Time is muscle - early intervention saves myocardium.

  11. ⚠ Patient Identification: Do NOT provide patient identifiers over the HEAR radio. Use COR for "Pre-Hospital STEMI Alert" with name and DOB.

  12. Refer to Death & Criteria for Withholding Resuscitation Poquoson Protocol if patient decompensates to cardiac arrest.

  13. Associated factors affecting hemodynamics (trauma, intoxication, illness) must be considered when determining medication administration.

  14. If law-enforcement restraints utilized, officer should accompany patient; consider exchanging for EMS-sanctioned restraints.

  15. Patients in law enforcement custody must have officer present at all times.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Cardiac - Hypertensive Crisis
docs.text: OVERVIEW: Hypertensive crisis is characterized by severely elevated blood pressure with potential for acute end-organ damage. Rapid assessment for associated life-threatening conditions (stroke, acute coronary syndrome, pulmonary edema) is essential, as management priorities depend on the patient's specific presentation and target organ involvement.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Systolic BP > 240 mmHg and/or diastolic BP > 120 mmHg
  • Chronic essential hypertension with sudden, unexplained, significant increase in blood pressure
  • History of missed antihypertensive medications
  • Recent cocaine or amphetamine use
  • History of kidney disease or renal failure
  • Pregnancy (preeclampsia/eclampsia risk)
  • Severe headache
  • Visual disturbances or blurred vision
  • Chest pain or pressure
  • Shortness of breath or respiratory distress
  • Altered mental status or confusion
  • Focal neurological deficits (weakness, numbness, speech difficulty)
  • Seizures
  • Nausea and vomiting
  • Hypertensive encephalopathy
  • Intracranial hemorrhage / Ischemic stroke
  • Acute coronary syndrome / Myocardial infarction
  • Acute pulmonary edema / CHF exacerbation
  • Acute aortic dissection
  • Acute renal failure
  • Preeclampsia / Eclampsia (if pregnant)
  • Substance-induced hypertensive emergency

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact:• Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact Poquoson Protocol.• Ensure scene safety and utilize appropriate PPE. • • 2 Primary Assessment:• Perform Primary and Secondary Survey.• Obtain complete set of vital signs including bilateral BP if possible.• Assess for signs of end-organ damage (neurological, cardiac, pulmonary, renal). • • 3 Stroke Assessment:• Perform stroke screening (Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale or equivalent).• If stroke is suspected, refer to Stroke / TIA Poquoson Protocol immediately.• Note time of symptom onset if applicable. • • 4 Cardiac Assessment:• Assess for chest pain, pressure, or symptoms consistent with acute coronary syndrome.• Obtain 12-lead ECG if available.• If ACS is suspected, refer to Chest Pain (Cardiac) Poquoson Protocol immediately. • • 5 Respiratory Assessment:• Assess for respiratory distress, pulmonary edema, or hypoxia.• Apply pulse oximetry and administer oxygen as indicated.• If respiratory distress is present, refer to Respiratory Distress Poquoson Protocol and Airway Management and Ventilatory Support Procedure. • • 6 Glucose Assessment:• Check blood glucose level.• If hyperglycemia or hypoglycemia is present or suspected, refer to Hyperglycemia / Hypoglycemia Poquoson Protocol.• Treat glucose abnormalities per appropriate protocol. • • 7 Medical Control Consultation:• Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for guidance on blood pressure management as needed.• Do NOT attempt to aggressively lower blood pressure in the field without specific orders.• Report all associated symptoms, vital signs, and suspected target organ involvement. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Cautious Blood Pressure Management: In most hypertensive emergencies, the goal is NOT to normalize blood pressure rapidly. Aggressive reduction can precipitate cerebral, coronary, or renal hypoperfusion. Specific blood pressure targets and medications should be determined by Poquoson OMD based on the underlying cause.

  2. ⚠ Acute End-Organ Assessment: Always assess for signs of acute end-organ damage including altered mental status, focal neurological deficits, chest pain, pulmonary edema, or acute renal failure. The presence of these findings defines a true hypertensive emergency requiring immediate intervention.

  3. ⚠ Aortic Dissection Suspicion: If acute aortic dissection is suspected (tearing chest/back pain, pulse deficits, new neurologic deficits), immediate Poquoson OMD notification is critical. These patients require specific management protocols and rapid transport to appropriate facilities.

  4. Referral-Based Protocol: This protocol is primarily a gateway to condition-specific management. The primary therapeutic interventions will occur through the referred protocols (Stroke, Chest Pain, Respiratory Distress, etc.) based on the patient's presentation and target organ involvement.

  5. Ischemic Stroke Consideration: In patients with suspected acute ischemic stroke, permissive hypertension may be appropriate to maintain cerebral perfusion. Do NOT lower blood pressure without specific Poquoson OMD direction.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
General - Post Resuscitation Care
docs.text: Guidelines for managing patients following Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC), focusing on hemodynamic support, airway management, and identifying reversible causes.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Cardiac arrest with ROSC
  • Hypotension
  • Hypoxia
  • Arrhythmias
  • Reversible causes: H's and T's
  • Target EtCO2 35-45 mmHg

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Airway management / Ventilatory support • • 2 Assess Blood Glucose (treat < 60 mg/dL per protocol) • • 3 Obtain / transmit 12-lead ECG (STEMI Triage if indicated) • • 4 Obtain IV/IO (Humeral IO preferred if IV unavailable) • 5 Fluid Bolus:ADULT: 0.9% Sodium Chloride up to 1 L IV/IO in 250 - 500 mL bolusesPEDIATRIC: 0.9% Sodium Chloride 20 mL/kg IV/IO (up to 60 mL/kg total) • 6 Maintain MAP > 65 mmHg:ADULT: Norepinephrine infusion 5 - 20 mcg/min IV/IOORADULT: Epinephrine infusion 2 - 10 mcg/min IV/IO • 7 Pediatric SBP Support:PEDIATRIC: Epinephrine infusion 0.1 - 1 mcg/kg/min IV/IO(Titrate to > 70 mmHg if < 1 yr, > 90 mmHg if > 1 yr. Max dose: 10 mcg/min) • 8 Bridge Therapy:ADULT: Push dose Epinephrine 5 - 10 mcg IV/IO every 2-3 minutes

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • Most patients will require at least some vasopressor support to maintain a MAP > 65 following cardiac arrest. • ⚠ Push dose Epinephrine is a bridge, not a replacement for a drip. • ⚠ Administer vasopressors through the largest, most proximal IV or IO & monitor for extravasation. • Maintain normal ventilation rate (target EtCO2 35-45 mmHg). • Identify and treat potentially reversible causes of cardiac arrest (H's and T's): Hypoxia, Hyperkalemia or hypokalemia, Hyperglycemia or hypoglycemia, Hypovolemia, Hydrogen Ion (acidosis), Tension pneumothorax/hemothorax, Tamponade (Cardiac), Thrombosis (Cardiac/pulmonary), Toxins, Trauma, Tablets (overdose).

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) is a rapid rhythm originating above the ventricles, characterized by a narrow QRS complex and rate typically exceeding 150 beats per minute. Management is stratified based on patient stability, with stable patients receiving vagal maneuvers and pharmacologic intervention while unstable patients require immediate synchronized cardioversion to restore perfusion.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Sudden onset palpitations
  • History of prior tachyarrhythmias
  • Medication history (including anticoagulants)
  • Congestive heart failure history
  • Recent illness or dehydration
  • Narrow complex tachycardia (Adult < 120 ms, Peds < 90 ms)
  • Rate usually > 150 bpm
  • Absent or abnormal P waves
  • Chest pain
  • Dyspnea
  • Altered mental status
  • Pulmonary edema
  • Signs of shock
  • Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
  • Atrial flutter
  • Ventricular tachycardia (for wide complex)
  • Sinus tachycardia
  • Accessory pathway mediated tachycardia (WPW)
  • Multifocal atrial tachycardia
  • Concurrent CHF or decreased ejection fraction

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact • • 2 Assess patient stability (chest pain, dyspnea, AMS, shock) • • 3 STABLE SVT PATHWAY 4 EMT: Obtain and transmit 12-Lead ECG • • 5 A: Obtain IV access • 6 A: 0.9% Sodium chloride up to 1 L IV/IO (Adult)250 – 500 mL boluses or 20 mL/kg IV/IO (Pediatric)Repeat as needed, max 60 mL/kg total • 7 I: If rhythm regular, Adenosine (Adenocard) 6 mg IV/IO (Adult)or 0.1 mg/kg IV/IO (Pediatric, max 6 mg)Followed by 20 mL (Adult) or 10–20 mL (Ped) 0.9% Sodium chloride flush • 8 I: If no conversion after 2 minutes, repeat Adenosine at DOUBLE initial dose • 9 I: If rhythm irregular/atrial fibrillation, initiate transportMonitor for signs of poor perfusion • 10 MC: Contact Poquoson OMD for guidanceEnsure 12-Lead ECG transmitted MC MC 11 UNSTABLE SVT PATHWAY 12 I: For light sedation, consider Midazolam (Versed)2.5 mg IV/IO or 5 mg IN/IM (Adult)0.05 mg/kg IV/IO or 0.1 mg/kg IM (Pediatric)Max: 2.5 mg IV/IO or 5 mg IM MC 13 I: Monitor SpO₂ and EtCO₂ during sedation 14 P: Perform SYNCHRONIZED cardioversion100 joules initial dose (Adult) or 1 joule/kg (Pediatric) 15 P: If unsuccessful, check pad placement and repeat at 200 joules (Adult)or 2 joules/kg (Pediatric) 16 P: If unsuccessful after two attempts, MC: Contact Poquoson OMD MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. Many patients who experience tachyarrhythmias have concurrent congestive heart failure or decreased ejection fraction. Exercise caution when providing IV fluids – give only enough to improve perfusion and monitor for signs of pulmonary edema.

  2. In rare cases, electrical cardioversion to sinus rhythm can result in embolization of small blood clots from the heart, leading to stroke. Following emergent cardioversion, monitor closely for changes in mental status or physical exam and reassess destination choice as appropriate.

  3. Dosing of electrical therapy described above serves as a guideline only. Confirm agency dosing for cardioversion and defibrillation with device specific manufacturers recommendations.

  4. ⚠ Ensure the device is set to SYNC prior to any attempted cardioversion of a patient with a palpable pulse. Unsynchronized cardioversion/defibrillation may lead to ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest.

  5. If the rhythm is polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (Torsades de Pointes), synchronization may not be possible. This is the only time a patient with a palpable pulse is treated with unsynchronized defibrillation.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
General - V-Fib / Pulseless V-Tach
docs.text: Guidelines for the management of pulseless ventricular tachycardia and ventricular fibrillation, emphasizing high-quality CPR and rapid defibrillation.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Unresponsive
  • Apneic
  • Pulseless
  • VF/VT on monitor or AED advise shock
  • Valid DDNR/Obvious death exclusions
  • Reversible causes

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Begin high-quality CPR / determine DNR status • • 2 Consider spinal motion restriction • • 3 Establish IV/IO (Humeral IO preferred) • 4 Fluid Resuscitation: 0.9% Sodium Chloride up to 1 L (Adult) / 20 mL/kg (Ped) • 5 Defibrillation: 200 J Biphasic / 360 J Monophasic (Adult) / 2 J/kg initial, 4 J/kg subsequent (Ped) (AED for EMR/EMT, Manual for I/P) • • 6 Epinephrine 1 mg (0.1mg/mL) IV/IO (Adult) / 0.01 mg/kg IV/IO (Ped) q3-5 mins • 7 First Antiarrhythmic: Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO (Adult) / 5 mg/kg (Ped max 300mg) OR Lidocaine 1 mg/kg IV/IO (Adult max 100mg, Ped max 50mg) • 8 Second Antiarrhythmic: Amiodarone 150 mg IV/IO (Adult) / [MC] 5 mg/kg (Ped max 150mg) OR Lidocaine 0.5 mg/kg IV/IO (Adult max 100mg) / 1 mg/kg (Ped max 50mg) • 9 Persistent VF/VT: Magnesium Sulfate 2 g IV/IO (Adult) / [MC] 40 mg/kg (Ped max 2g) • 10 Dialysis/Crush/Hyperkalemia: Calcium Chloride 1 g IV/IO (Adult) / 20 mg/kg (Ped max 1g) THEN flush THEN Sodium Bicarbonate 50 mEq IV/IO (Adult) / 1 mEq/kg (Ped max 50 mEq) • 11 Proceed to Post ROSC Care if pulses return • • 12 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for guidance / termination of resuscitation MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • High-quality, uninterrupted CPR is the most important intervention in cardiac arrest. • Ensure optimal defibrillator pad placement. • ⚠ Flush lines well between Calcium Chloride and Sodium Bicarbonate to prevent precipitation. • Reversible causes (H's and T's).

docs.title: MASTER AI SYSTEM PROMPT: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Protocols
docs.text: Role: You are an expert EMS Medical Editor and Protocol Developer. Objective: Adapt regional PEMS protocols into the Poquoson Fire and Rescue ODEMSA-style matrix format.

docs.title: 1. Core Directives
docs.text:
• Clinical Strictness: NEVER alter medications, dosages, indications, contraindications, or clinical algorithms from the source text. The clinical standard of care must remain identical to PEMS.
  • Formatting Style: Use the ODEMSA visual matrix layout (Markdown tables) for all interventions and assessments.
  • Tone: Authoritative, clear, highly organized, and optimized for quick field reference.

docs.title: 2. Global Text Replacements
docs.text: Automatically replace the following terms in the source text: * Peninsula EMS, PEMS, Peninsula EMS Council ➔ Poquoson Fire and Rescue * PEMS Medical Advisory Committee, Regional Medical Director ➔ Poquoson OMD * Regional Protocol(s) ➔ Poquoson Protocol(s)

docs.title: 3. Required Protocol Structure
docs.text: Every clinical protocol must follow this exact Markdown structure and order:

docs.title: A. Header
docs.text:
# Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
## [Category] - [Protocol Name]

docs.title: B. Overview
docs.text: A brief, 2-3 sentence high-level summary of the emergency, pathophysiology, or primary goal of the protocol.

docs.title: C. Assessment & Criteria (3-Column Table)
docs.text:
Extract the criteria and assessment points into a 3-column Markdown table. 

### Assessment & Criteria
=== "History of Present Illness"
    - [Bullet points]

=== "Signs & Symptoms"
    - [Bullet points]

=== "Differential Considerations"
    - [Bullet points]

docs.title: D. Interventions (Matrix Table)
docs.text: Translate the sequential treatment steps into a 6-column matrix. * Columns: #, Action / Intervention, EMR, EMT, A, I, P * Rows: Sequential steps of care. * Indicators: * Use a bullet point (•) if the provider level is authorized to perform the intervention as a standing order. * Leave the cell blank if the provider level is NOT authorized. * Use MC if the provider must contact Medical Control prior to the intervention. * Formatting within the table: Bold all medication names and dosages. Use <br> for line breaks within table cells.

docs.title: E. Pearls
docs.text: A numbered list of important considerations, warnings, and tips. * Use the caution symbol (⚠) at the beginning of the bullet point for critical warnings or contraindications.

docs.title: Patient Care Protocols, Policies, and Procedures
docs.text:
Effective: 05/01/2026 Approved by: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Operational Medical Director (OMD)

Poquoson Fire and Rescue 101 City Hall Avenue Poquoson, VA 23662

Phone: 757-868-3510 Email: Christopher.Wolkowich@poquoson-va.gov Website: https://www.ci.poquoson.va.us/177/Fire-Rescue

docs.title: Acknowledgements
docs.text:
This document was originally developed by the Peninsula's EMS (PEMS) Council. Poquoson Fire and Rescue extends its gratitude to the PEMS Medical Advisory Committee and the regional partners who established the foundational evidence-based guidelines utilized in this document.

These protocols have been adopted, adapted, and approved by the Poquoson Fire and Rescue Operational Medical Director to serve the specific operational and clinical needs of our agency and the citizens of Poquoson.

docs.title: Protocols, Policies, and Procedures Mission Statement
docs.text: This document aims to provide current, well-researched, and accepted standards with the ultimate goal of minimizing the morbidity and mortality of our patients in Poquoson. It provides definitive guidelines for the treatment of specific emergency conditions in the pre-hospital setting by Poquoson Fire and Rescue personnel.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
• To establish minimum standards for appropriate patient care within Poquoson Fire and Rescue.
  • To ensure an accountability structure for our Operational Medical Director, EMS leadership, and field providers.
  • To provide a continuous quality improvement framework for our paramedic and EMT resources.

docs.title: Scope of Practice
docs.text:
These protocols apply exclusively to EMS providers operating under the medical direction of the Poquoson Fire and Rescue OMD. Providers are expected to utilize these protocols in conjunction with their clinical judgment, training, and the principles of Conscious Vigilance in patient care.

Document maintained by Poquoson Fire and Rescue. For questions or clarifications, contact the OMD.

docs.title: Levels of Certification
docs.text: The protocols use the following abbreviations to indicate provider levels: * EMR: Emergency Medical Responder * EMT: Emergency Medical Technician * A: Advanced Emergency Medical Technician * I: Intermediate * P: Paramedic

docs.title: Special Requirements
docs.text:
These items in the provider's scope of practice have the following special requirements:

  1. Documentation of EMS agency (post-affiliation) training, to the specific skills and medications, must be present and maintained in the provider's personnel file, and

  2. EMS agency Operational Medical Director must provide specific written authorization, to include and/or limit, these specific skills and medications, in the provider's scope of practice. Written authorization must be kept and maintained in the provider's personnel file.

docs.title: Understanding the Protocol Format
docs.text: The following are your new protocols. These have been formatted to maximize clarity and speed of use in the field. The new layout utilizes a matrix-style grid system, allowing providers to quickly scan down the page and immediately identify the interventions authorized for their specific certification level.

docs.title: The Intervention Matrix
docs.text:
Instead of listing paragraphs of text for each certification level, interventions are listed sequentially in a table. To determine your authorized actions, locate your certification level at the top of the columns (EMR, EMT, A, I, P) and read down the grid.

# Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Perform general patient management. • • 2 Administer Epinephrine Auto-injector • • 3 Obtain IV/IO access. • 4 Administer Epinephrine Infusion MC

docs.title: Matrix Key & Symbols:
docs.text:
• Dot (•): You are authorized to perform that intervention as a Standing Order.
  • Blank Space: The intervention is NOT within your scope of practice or protocol authorization.
  • MC: You must contact Medical Control for authorization prior to performing that intervention.
  • Caution (⚠): Special considerations, critical safety warnings, or contraindications apply to this step.

docs.title: Protocol Structure
docs.text:
• Overview: A brief summary of the emergency or condition.
