  • Assessment & Criteria: A 3-column table breaking down the History of Present Illness (HPI), Signs and Symptoms, and Considerations (Differential Diagnoses) to guide your clinical judgment.
  • Interventions: The sequential matrix of patient care.
  • PEARLS Section: Each protocol contains a PEARLS section providing important considerations, contraindications, and points for taking your patient care from mediocre to exceptional.

Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols Effective: 05/01/2026 Version: Based on PEMS March 2024 / Adapted for Poquoson Fire and Rescue

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
General - Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact
docs.text: OVERVIEW: The Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol applies to all patients and is the foundation for all patient care. It allows other protocols to focus on specific goals for patient management. While the steps listed below generally occur in order, the dynamics of an emergency scene may dictate a change to the sequence.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text: History of Present Illness (HPI) Signs and Symptoms Considerations (Differential) • Obtain chief complaint• Determine mechanism of injury or nature of illness• Obtain patient history and medication list • Assess baseline vital signs• Perform Primary and Secondary Survey• Assess Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) • AEIOU TIPS (See Pearls for Altered Mental Status)• SMASHED 2 (See Pearls for Altered Mental Status)• Consider underlying medical vs. traumatic etiology

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Scene Safety / PPE / Patient Contact:• Utilize appropriate agencies and resources as needed (lift assistance, ALS, law enforcement).• Bring all necessary equipment to the patient’s side. • • 2 Initial Assessment:• Perform Primary and Secondary Survey as indicated.• Consider spinal motion restriction if indicated by MOI or history. • • 3 Ensure Adequate Airway, Oxygenation, and Ventilation:• Manage airway by the least invasive means necessary.• Maintain home oxygen unless symptoms indicate need for increase.• Administer up to 15 LPM oxygen through an appropriate delivery device. • • 4 Vital Signs:• Obtain BP, pulse rate, respirations, and GCS.• Consider obtaining blood glucose level and temperature.• Reassess Stable patients at least every 15 minutes.• Reassess Unstable patients at least every 5 minutes. • • 5 Assess SpO2:• Consider assessing EtCO2 and/or SpCO as indicated. • • 6 Consider 12-Lead ECG:• Acquire within 5 minutes of patient contact.• Transmit to receiving facility within 10 minutes of patient contact. • • 7 Medication Administration:• Confirm all "Rights" (Patient, Medication, Dose, Route, Time).• Ensure medications are not expired.• Assess for drug-drug interactions and contraindications. • • 8 Establish IV / IO access:• Establish access when indicated. • 9 Consider continuous cardiac monitoring. • 10 Transport:• Base transport decisions on clinical condition and regional Field Triage policies.• Deliver a pre-arrival report to the receiving facility as soon as practical.• Consult with Medical Control regarding destination or clinical guidance. • • 11 Refer to relevant protocols:• If no protocol applies, or for clinical guidance at any time, contact Medical Control. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. Infant Age Guidelines:
  2. Newborn: Up to 48 hours following birth.

  3. Neonates: Up to 28 days following birth.

  4. Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS):

  5. Eye Opening: (4) Spontaneous, (3) To voice, (2) To pain, (1) None
  6. Verbal: (5) Oriented, (4) Confused, (3) Inappropriate words, (2) Incomprehensible sounds, (1) None

  7. Motor: (6) Obeys commands, (5) Localizes pain, (4) Withdraws to pain, (3) Abnormal flexion (Decorticate), (2) Extension to pain (Decerebrate), (1) None

  8. Altered Mental Status Differential Diagnosis (AEIOU TIPS):

  9. A: Alcohol
  10. E: Endocrine, Encephalopathy, Electrolytes
  11. I: Insulin (hypoglycemia / hyperglycemia)
  12. O: Oxygen (hypoxia), Opiates (drugs of abuse)
  13. U: Uremia
  14. T: Toxins, Trauma, Temperature (hyperthermia / hypothermia)
  15. I: Infection
  16. P: Psychiatric, Porphyria

  17. S: Stroke, Shock, Subarachnoid/Intracranial Hemorrhage, Space-occupying CNS Lesion, Seizure

  18. Altered Mental Status Differential Diagnosis (SMASHED 2):

  19. S: Substrates (Glucose high/low, thiamine deficiency), Sepsis
  20. M: Meningitis (CNS infection, dementia, encephalitis), Mental illness (Acute psychosis, mania, depression)
  21. A: Alcohol (Intoxication, withdrawal), Accident (Head trauma, CVA, cerebral contusion, hemorrhage)
  22. S: Seizure (Active or post-ictal), Stimulants (Cocaine, amphetamines, PCP, LSD, ketamine)
  23. H: Hyper- (Hypertension, hyperthyroidism, hypercarbia, hyperthermia), Hypo- (Hypotension, hypothyroidism, hypoxia, hypothermia)
  24. E: Electrolytes (Sodium, calcium, potassium), Encephalopathy (Hepatic, uremic, hypertensive)
  25. D: Drugs (Intoxication or withdrawal), Don’t forget other drugs

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines for managing agitated or combative patients, emphasizing de-escalation, identifying underlying causes, and appropriate chemical restraint.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Psychiatric, medical, or traumatic emergency
  • Agitated, aggressive, or combative behavior
  • Risk of harm to self or others
  • Agitation Levels:
  • Mild: Anxious, apprehensive demeanor but non-aggressive
  • Moderate: Pulling at tubes/catheters or aggressive behavior without violence
  • Severe: Overt combativeness, violence, or self-harm / immediate danger to self or staff
  • Potential medical or traumatic causes (SMASHED2)
  • Patients less than 25 kg who cannot be safely managed with de-escalation require Poquoson OMD / Medical Control guidance

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Assess capacity and treat potential underlying causes of agitation / altered mental status. Refer to administrative policy Patient Refusals. • • 2 Apply de-escalation techniques. Remain calm, maintain soothing tone, allow space, do not reinforce delusions, set boundaries. • • 3 Apply least restrictive physical restraints necessary. Refer to administrative policy Patient Restraint. • • 4 As soon as feasible, assess and initiate continuous monitoring of cardiac rhythm. • 5 MODERATE AGITATIONAdminister Haloperidol (Haldol) 5 mg IV/IO slow push or IM(Pediatric: 0.02 mg/kg IV/IO slow push or 0.05 mg/kg IM. Max dose: 1 mg IV/IO, 2.5 mg IM)(Use half dose 2.5 mg IV/IO or IM for frail, hypotensive, underweight <50kg, and elderly >65yrs)ANDAdminister Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 50 mg IV/IO slow push or IM(Pediatric: 1 mg/kg IV/IO slow push or IM. Max dose 50 mg) • 6 SEVERE AGITATION (not responding to above)Administer Midazolam (Versed) 5 mg IV/IO slow push or 10 mg IM(Pediatric: 0.05 mg/kg IV/IO slow push or 0.1 mg/kg IN/IM. Max dose: 2.5 mg IV/IO, 5 mg IN/IM)(Use half dose 2.5 mg IV/IO or 5 mg IM for frail, hypotensive, underweight <50kg, and elderly >65yrs)⚠ Do not mix Haloperidol and Midazolam in the same syringe. • 7 IMMINENT HARM (Severely agitated with imminent risk of harm to self or others)Administer Ketamine (Ketalar) 4 mg/kg IMBe prepared to manage airway and support ventilation as needed. 8 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for guidance if unable to safely restrain or transport the patient. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Never place the patient prone or in any position which compromises breathing, circulation, or constricts the neck or chest.
  2. ⚠ Patients in law enforcement custody or under arrest must have a law enforcement officer present at all times.
  3. ⚠ Use of chemical restraint or sedating medications solely to facilitate an arrest by law enforcement is prohibited.
  4. Use of appropriate de-escalation techniques should take precedence over physical restraint or pharmacologic management whenever possible.
  5. Mild agitation = anxious, apprehensive demeanor but non-aggressive.
  6. Moderate agitation = pulling at tubes/catheters or aggressive behavior without violence.
  7. Severe agitation = overt combativeness, violence or self-harm / immediate danger to self or staff.
  8. Consider potential medical or traumatic causes (SMASHED2).

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
General - Cold Exposure (Environmental)
docs.text: Guidelines for managing systemic hypothermia and localized cold injuries, emphasizing gentle handling and prevention of further heat loss.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • High-risk patients: very young or old, immobilized (e.g., prolonged entrapment), alcohol use, trauma/significant burns.
  • High-risk environment: cold, windy environments, acute immersion incident.
  • CNS depression
  • Cardiac dysrhythmias
  • Abnormal vital signs
  • Assess pulse and respiratory rate for at least 60 seconds.

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow General – Universal Patient Care/Initial Patient Contact Protocol. • • 2 Remove all wet clothing, place in a warm environment, prevent further heat loss, and replace with warm blankets. • • 3 Handle the patient gently and avoid excessive movement (risk of cardiac arrest/VF). • • 4 Apply warmed and humidified O2 if available. • • 5 Localized cold injury: Apply loose, sterile dressings to the affected part. • • 6 If patient is alert and responsive: Use active external rewarming with heat packs, warm blankets, etc. • • 7 Cardiac Arrest: Make persistent attempts at resuscitation for victims of prolonged cold exposure. Begin CPR and continuous warming procedures. • • 8 Rapid transport immediately with resuscitation efforts continued en route. Do not delay transport waiting for ALS. • • 9 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control prior to use of AED. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Hypothermia patients are fragile: Rough handling, IV insertion, intubation, etc. might cause VF that is refractory to defibrillation and antiarrhythmic agents- use AHA guidelines for hypothermic arrest.
  2. ⚠ DO NOT place heat packs, hot water bottles, IV bags, or other heat-retaining devices directly on skin.
  3. ⚠ In Localized injuries DO NOT: Allow patient to use the affected part, rub the affected part, expose the part to direct dry heat, immerse the part in snow or hot water, attempt to debride blisters.
  4. ⚠ During cardiac arrest, medications should be spaced at longer intervals.
  5. Remove jewelry and constricting items.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines for managing acute submersion, non-fatal drowning, and decompression injuries, emphasizing airway management, rewarming, and specialized transport.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Submersion
  • Compressed air diving
  • Altered mental status (AMS)
  • Seizures
  • Vision changes
  • Respiratory distress
  • Hemoptysis
  • Chest pain
  • Headache
  • Focal neurologic signs
  • Ataxia
  • Pain in joints / extremities
  • Hypothermia
  • Pulmonary barotrauma
  • Arterial gas embolism
  • Decompression sickness

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol. Remove the patient from the water. • • 2 Consider spinal motion restriction. Refer to Spinal Motion Restriction procedure. • • 3 Assess airway and ventilation. Refer to Airway Management and Ventilatory Support protocol. • • 4 Remove wet clothing and dry the skin. Keep the patient warm. • • 5 If hypothermia and hemodynamically unstable, focus effort on rewarming as well as resuscitation simultaneously. • • 6 Diving emergencies: Assess for pulmonary barotrauma, decompression sickness, and/or arterial gas embolism. Refer to appropriate Poquoson Protocol(s) as needed. • • 7 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for hyperbaric transport destination guidance if decompression injury is suspected. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Do not initiate transport to a hyperbaric center without confirming chamber availability first.
  2. ⚠ In hypothermia with hemodynamic instability, focus on both rewarming and resuscitation simultaneously.
  3. Hyperbaric Centers info: Sentara Leigh Hospital ED (Norfolk, VA): 757-261-6804 / Diver Alert Network: (919) 684-9111.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines for managing heat-related illnesses, distinguishing between heat exhaustion and heat stroke, and initiating rapid cooling and fluid resuscitation.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Extreme heat exposure
  • CNS depression
  • Arrhythmias
  • Hot/dry vs normal skin
  • Heat stroke vs exhaustion
  • Hypovolemia

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Place patient in cool environment • • 2 Remove excess clothing • • 3 Normal skin temp: Apply cool compresses • • 4 Hot skin temp (Heat Stroke): Treat as life-threatening/start rapid cooling (cool mist, ice packs to armpits/groin/neck, wet sheet) • • 5 Shivering: Slow the cooling process • • 6 Suspected heat stroke/hypovolemia: 0.9% Normal Saline 20 mL/kg up to 1000 mL bolus IV/IO • 7 Continuously reassess patient • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Hot skin to touch indicates a life-threatening emergency (Heat Stroke) - begin rapid cooling immediately.
  2. ⚠ If patient begins to shiver, slow the cooling process as shivering generates heat.
  3. ⚠ Continuously reassess fluid needs during resuscitation.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines for assessing and managing pain using pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic methods while maintaining hemodynamic stability.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Any patient with discomfort/pain caused by injury or illness
  • Assess pain using 0-10 scale
  • Use Wong-Baker (FACES) chart or FLACC scale for non-verbal patients (including infants and children)
  • Blood Pressure Minimums (Ensure patient is not hypotensive):
  • Infants 0–12 months: SBP > 70 mmHg
  • Children 1–10 years: SBP > 80 mmHg
  • Children & Adults > 10 years: SBP > 100 mmHg
  • Opiate side effects (respiratory depression, CNS depression, bradycardia, hypotension)
  • Associated factors affecting hemodynamics (trauma, intoxication, illness)
  • Nausea

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol. • • 2 Assess baseline pain scale. Consider non-pharmacologic options (reassurance, distraction, positioning, splinting, cold therapy) first or as an adjunct. • • 3 Assess and monitor blood pressure to ensure patient is not hypotensive (see criteria). • • 4 Nausea:• Adult: Ondansetron 4 mg IV/IM/PO• Pediatric [> 1 year]: Ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg IV/IM/PO (Max dose: 4 mg) • 5 Mild to Moderate Pain (2 - 5):• Adult: Ketorolac 15 mg IV / 30 mg IM • 6 Moderate to Severe Pain (6 - 10):• Adult: Fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV/IN/IM (Max total dose: 100 mcg)• Pediatric: Fentanyl 0.5-1 mcg/kg IV/IN/IM (Max dose: 25 mcg, Max total dose: 100 mcg) • 7 OR Moderate to Severe Pain (6 - 10):• Adult: Morphine Sulfate 2.5-5 mg IV/IM (Max total dose: 10 mg)• Pediatric: Morphine Sulfate 0.1 mg/kg IV/IM (Max dose: 2.5 mg, Max total dose: 10 mg) • 8 If unable to adequately control pain or safely and humanely extricate/transport a stable patient, contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for guidance. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ Opiates cause respiratory/CNS depression, bradycardia, and/or hypotension - monitor vitals closely following any administration.
  • ⚠ Use lower opiate doses (25 mcg Fentanyl or 2.5 mg Morphine) for frail, low weight (<50 kg), opiate naïve, or elderly (> 65 yr) adult patients.
  • ⚠ Administer Naloxone if respiratory depression occurs. Refer to Overdose / Poisoning / Toxic Ingestions Poquoson Protocol.
  • The goal of analgesia is not to completely alleviate pain, but to reduce pain to a manageable level which allows for safe and humane treatment and transport.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines for the pharmacological facilitation of airway management and post-intubation sedation in patients who are not hypotensive.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Need for airway management / intubation
  • Intact gag reflex
  • Agitation
  • Hypoxia
  • Respiratory Failure
  • Blood Pressure minimums:   • Infants 0 – 12 months: SBP > 70 mmHg   • Children 1 – 10 years: SBP > 80 mmHg   • Children & adults > 10 years: SBP > 100 mmHg
  • Asthma/COPD vs ACS

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol. Provide supplemental oxygen and/or ventilatory support. Refer to Airway Management and Ventilatory Support procedure. • • 2 Assess and monitor blood pressure. Ensure patient is not hypotensive. • • 3 Establish IV/IO access. • 4 Pre-Intubation Sedation, consider ONE of the following:• ADULT: Ketamine (Ketalar) 1.5 - 2 mg/kg IV/IO (Maximum single dose: 200 mg) OR 4 - 5 mg/kg IM/IN (Maximum single dose: 400 mg). If administering IV, administer over 1-2 minutes.• PEDIATRIC: Ketamine (Ketalar) 1 - 2 mg/kg IV/IO (Maximum single dose: 50 mg) OR 2 - 5 mg/kg IM/IN (Maximum single dose: 100 mg). If administering IV, administer over 1-2 minutes.OR• ADULT: Midazolam (Versed) 2.5 - 5 mg IV/IO (Maximum single dose: 5 mg) OR 5 - 10 mg IN/IM (Maximum single dose: 10 mg).• PEDIATRIC: Midazolam (Versed) 0.1 mg/kg IV/IO (Maximum single dose: 2.5 mg) OR 0.2 mg/kg IN/IM (Maximum single dose: 5 mg). • 5 Post-Intubation Sedation, consider ONE of the following:• ADULT: Ketamine (Ketalar) 1 mg/kg IV/IO (Maximum single dose: 100 mg). May repeat every 10-15 minutes as needed.• PEDIATRIC: Ketamine (Ketalar) 0.5 mg/kg IV/IO (Maximum single dose: 25 mg). May repeat every 10-15 minutes as needed. • 6 OR Post-Intubation Sedation (Midazolam / Fentanyl):• ADULT: Midazolam (Versed) 2.5 mg IV/IO. May repeat every 10-15 minutes as needed. (Maximum total dose: 10 mg)• PEDIATRIC: Midazolam (Versed) 0.05 mg/kg IV/IO (Maximum single dose: 2.5 mg). May repeat every 10-15 minutes as needed. (Maximum total dose: 5 mg)AND/OR• ADULT: Fentanyl (Sublimaze) 25 - 50 mcg IV/IO. May repeat every 10-15 minutes as needed. (Maximum total dose: 150 mcg)• PEDIATRIC: Fentanyl (Sublimaze) 0.5 - 1 mcg/kg IV/IO. May repeat every 10-15 minutes as needed. (Maximum total dose: 50 mcg) •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ Concurrent use of Fentanyl and Midazolam significantly increases the risk of hypotension and respiratory depression. • ⚠ Ketamine should be avoided in patients with suspected myocardial ischemia or infarction due to its sympathomimetic effects (increased heart rate and blood pressure). • ⚠ Consider the use of lower doses of sedatives in frail, elderly, or opiate/benzodiazepine-naive patients. • Ketamine is the preferred agent for sedation in patients with reactive airway disease (asthma/COPD) due to its bronchodilatory effects. • Ensure adequate sedation is maintained during transport to prevent unplanned extubation or patient discomfort.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Medical - Allergic Reaction / Anaphylaxis
docs.text: OVERVIEW: This protocol outlines the management of patients experiencing an acute allergic reaction or severe anaphylaxis. A mild-to-moderate allergic reaction typically involves localized symptoms, while anaphylaxis is a severe, systemic, and potentially life-threatening event involving rapid onset of mucosal/skin, respiratory, cardiovascular, or gastrointestinal compromise.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Exposure to a known or likely allergen (e.g., insect sting, food, medication)
  • Rapid onset of symptoms following exposure
  • Mild/Moderate: Localized signs or symptoms of an acute allergic reaction
  • Anaphylaxis (Severe): Involvement of skin or mucosal tissue
  • Respiratory distress
  • Hypotension or syncope
  • Persistent gastrointestinal symptoms (abdominal cramps, diarrhea, vomiting)
  • Two or more severe symptoms rapidly after exposure to a likely allergen indicates anaphylaxis
  • Hypotension alone after exposure to a known allergen indicates anaphylaxis

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Universal Patient Care:• Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol. • • 2 Stinger Removal:• Remove stinger if present. Do not squeeze stinger. Scrape it away with a flat surface. • • Acute Allergic Reaction (non-Anaphylaxis) 3 Antihistamine Administration:• ADULT: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 50 mg IV/IO slow push or IM.• PEDIATRIC: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 1 mg/kg IV/IO slow push or IM (Maximum dose: 50 mg). • 4 Bronchodilator (Wheezing):• If wheezing is present, administer:• ADULT & PEDIATRIC: Albuterol sulfate (Proventil) 3 mL of 0.083% solution, nebulized. Repeat as needed for wheezing. • 5 Anticholinergic (Wheezing):• If wheezing is present, administer:• ADULT & PEDIATRIC: Ipratropium bromide (Atrovent) 3 mL of 0.02% solution, nebulized, once. • 6 Corticosteroid (Consideration):• Consider administering:• ADULT: Methylprednisolone Succinate (Solu-Medrol) 125 mg slow IV push.• PEDIATRIC: Methylprednisolone Succinate (Solu-Medrol) 2 mg/kg slow IV push (Maximum dose: 125 mg). • Anaphylaxis 7 Epinephrine Administration:• Administer IM using either dose-specific clearly marked or color-coded syringe.• ADULT: Epinephrine autoinjector or 0.3 mg [1 mg/mL] IM.• PEDIATRIC (< 30 kg): Pediatric Epinephrine autoinjector or 0.15 mg [1 mg/mL] IM.• PEDIATRIC (> 30 kg): Epinephrine autoinjector or 0.3 mg [1 mg/mL] IM.• Repeat every 5-15 minutes for up to 3 injections as needed. • • 8 Vascular Access:• Obtain IV/IO access. • 9 Fluid Resuscitation (Hypotension):• If patient is hypotensive, administer:• ADULT: 0.9% Sodium chloride up to 1 L IV/IO in 250 – 500 mL boluses.• PEDIATRIC: 0.9% Sodium chloride 20 mL/kg IV/IO.• Repeat as needed to maintain adequate perfusion, up to 60 mL/kg total. • 10 Antihistamine Administration:• ADULT: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 50 mg IV/IO slow push or IM.• PEDIATRIC: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 1 mg/kg IV/IO slow push or IM (Maximum dose: 50 mg). • 11 Bronchodilator (Wheezing):• If wheezing is present, administer:• ADULT & PEDIATRIC: Albuterol sulfate (Proventil) 3 mL of 0.083% solution, nebulized. May repeat as needed. • 12 Anticholinergic (Wheezing):• If wheezing is present, administer:• ADULT & PEDIATRIC: Ipratropium bromide (Atrovent) 3 mL of 0.02% solution, nebulized, once. • 13 Corticosteroid Administration:• ADULT: Methylprednisolone succinate (Solu-Medrol) 125 mg IV/IO slow push.• PEDIATRIC: Methylprednisolone succinate (Solu-Medrol) 2 mg/kg IV/IO slow push (Maximum dose: 125 mg). •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ EMTs must use either dose-specific clearly marked or color-coded syringes for Epinephrine IM.
  2. ⚠ Epinephrine is the definitive treatment for anaphylaxis and should be administered immediately. Delaying Epinephrine administration can lead to poor outcomes.
  3. Anaphylaxis requires two or more rapid onset symptoms (skin/mucosal, respiratory distress, hypotension, GI symptoms) after exposure to a likely allergen, or hypotension alone after exposure to a known allergen.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: This protocol outlines the assessment and management of patients presenting with hypotension and shock not related to major trauma. The goal is to rapidly identify the underlying cause, restore adequate perfusion, and maintain oxygenation to vital organs.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • No evidence of acute traumatic injury (Refer to Poquoson Trauma protocol for patients with injuries)
  • Recent illness (infection, sepsis)
  • History of cardiac disease
  • Vomiting, diarrhea, or poor fluid intake
  • Under 1 year of age: SBP less than 70 mmHg or MAP less than 55 mmHg
  • Over 1 year of age: SBP less than 90 mmHg or MAP less than 65 mmHg
  • Altered mental status
  • Diaphoresis
  • Chest pain or discomfort
  • Syncope
  • Dyspnea
  • Pulmonary edema
  • Cardiogenic shock (Pump problem: dysrhythmia, ischemia, tamponade)
  • Distributive shock (Tank problem: sepsis, anaphylaxis)
  • Hypovolemic shock (Tank problem: hemorrhage, dehydration)
  • Obstructive shock (Pipe problem: pulmonary embolism, aortic dissection)

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol. • • 2 Obtain relevant history and perform a focused assessment to determine the potential cause.* • • 3 Maintain SpO2 94 – 98%. Refer to Airway Management and Ventilatory Support procedure. • • 4 Obtain and transmit a 12-Lead ECG. • • 5 Obtain IV/IO access. Administer:ADULT: 0.9% Sodium chloride up to 1 L IV/IO in 250 – 500 mL boluses.PEDIATRIC: 0.9% Sodium chloride 20 mL/kg IV/IO.Repeat as needed to maintain adequate perfusion, up to 60 mL/kg** total. • 6 Assess and continuously monitor cardiac rhythm. Refer to Poquoson Tachyarrhythmias and Symptomatic Bradycardia protocols as needed. • 7 Maintain mean arterial pressure (MAP) above 65 mmHg. Consider:ADULT: Norepinephrine (Levophed) infusion 5 – 20 mcg/min IV/IO (Add 4 mg of Norepinephrine to 250 mL 0.9% Sodium chloride = 16 mcg/mL)ORADULT: Epinephrine infusion 2 – 10 mcg/min IV/IO (Add 1 mg of Epinephrine [1 mg/mL] to 100 mL of 0.9% Sodium chloride = 10 mcg/mL) • 8 Maintain age adjusted SBP. Consider:PEDIATRIC: Epinephrine 0.1 - 1 mcg/kg/min IV/IO infusion. Max dose: 10 mcg/min.Under 1 year old: Titrate to SBP > 70 mmHgOver 1 year old: Titrate to SBP > 90 mmHg(Add 1 mg of Epinephrine [1 mg/mL] to 100 mL of 0.9% Sodium chloride = 10 mcg/mL)Note: Infusion via IV pump is preferred if available. Monitor closely for signs of extravasation. • 9 To maintain MAP > 65 mmHg until a vasopressor infusion is prepared, consider:ADULT: Push dose Epinephrine 5-10 mcg IV/IO every 2-3 minutes(Add 100 mcg Epinephrine (1 mL of 0.1 mg/mL) to 9 mL of 0.9% Normal Saline = 10 mcg / mL) 10 Contact Medical Control for guidance as needed. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • This protocol is not intended for the management of patients with hypotension due to major trauma. • * There are many potential causes of hypotension. Generally, the causes fall into one of four categories: cardiogenic, distributive, hypovolemic or obstructive. Alternatively, hypotension can be rapidly assessed as a: - Pump problem: Cardiac issues such as dysrhythmia, ischemia, or tamponade - Tank problem: Hypovolemia (intravascular) due to hemorrhage, vomiting, diarrhea, dehydration, sepsis or anaphylaxis - Pipe problem: Large vessel injury or obstruction such as aortic dissection or pulmonary embolism ⚠ Hypotension is a late sign of shock in pediatric patients. Children with tachycardia should be assessed closely for signs of compensated shock and providers must maintain a high index of suspicion that they may progress into decompensated shock with little warning. • When administering fluids to an infant or child, use a three-way stopcock and syringe to pull/push the desired volume. ⚠ ** Use caution when administering IV fluids to patients with known or suspect congestive heart failure or decreased ejection fraction. Large volumes of rapidly administered fluids may lead to pulmonary edema and respiratory distress. Administer smaller boluses (250 mL then reassess) and consider early administration of vasopressors to patients unlikely to tolerate large volumes of IV fluids. ⚠ Administer vasopressors through the largest, most proximal IV site available (AC or higher preferable) or IO. Verify patency prior to initiating infusion and monitor closely for signs of extravasation. ⚠ Use caution when considering positive pressure ventilation for patients with hypotension, particularly due to intravascular hypovolemia (reduced pre-load). Intubation and positive pressure ventilation may lead to a sudden drop in blood pressure or circulatory collapse / cardiac arrest.

docs.title: Norepinephrine (Levophed) Drip Chart
docs.text:
To mix, add 4 mg Norepinephrine to 250 mL 0.9% Sodium Chloride Final Concentration: **16 mcg/mL

Dose Drops per minute with 60-drop set 5 mcg/min 19 6 mcg/min 23 7 mcg/min 26 8 mcg/min 30 9 mcg/min 34 10 mcg/min 38 12.5 mcg/min 47 15 mcg/min 56 17.5 mcg/min 65 20 mcg/min 75

docs.title: Epinephrine Drip Chart
docs.text:
To mix, add 1 mg (1 mg/mL) Epinephrine to 100 mL 0.9% Sodium Chloride Final Concentration: **10 mcg/mL

Dose Drops per minute with 60-drop set 1 mcg/min 6 2 mcg/min 12 3 mcg/min 18 4 mcg/min 24 5 mcg/min 30 6 mcg/min 36 7 mcg/min 42 8 mcg/min 48 9 mcg/min 54 10 mcg/min 60

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Medical - OB Emergencies
docs.text: This protocol outlines the assessment and management of obstetrical emergencies, including normal childbirth, breech presentations, umbilical cord prolapse, eclampsia (seizures), and third-trimester bleeding such as uterine rupture and abruptio placenta.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Gestational age and expected due date
  • Number of previous pregnancies (gravida) and deliveries (para)
  • Onset, frequency, and duration of contractions
  • Presence of vaginal bleeding or fluid discharge
  • Recent trauma or medical complications
  • Labor: Regular contractions, crowning, urge to push
  • Pre-eclampsia: BP > 160/110 mmHg, severe headache, visual disturbances, edema
  • Third-Trimester Bleeding: Painless or painful vaginal bleeding
  • Eclampsia: Active seizures during pregnancy or postpartum
  • Normal labor vs. false labor
  • Breech or abnormal presentation
  • Umbilical cord prolapse
  • Placenta previa vs. Abruptio placenta
  • Uterine rupture

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care, don appropriate PPE, and visualize perineum for crowning or presenting part. • • 2 Normal Delivery: Apply gentle pressure on infant's skull to prevent explosive delivery. Check for nuchal cord. Dry, warm, and stimulate the neonate. DO NOT perform routine airway suctioning. • • 3 Shoulder Dystocia: Place mother in McRobert's position (knees to chest) and apply suprapubic pressure. • • 4 Umbilical Cord Prolapse: Wrap exposed cord in saline-moistened gauze. Elevate mother's hips or place in Trendelenburg position. Insert gloved hand to relieve pressure on the cord. DO NOT attempt vaginal delivery. • • 5 Breech Delivery: Allow controlled, spontaneous delivery to the umbilicus. Deliver the legs and support the body. • • 6 Eclampsia (Seizures): Administer Magnesium Sulfate 4 g IV/IO infusion over 10 minutes. • 7 If seizures persist > 5 minutes, administer Midazolam 5 mg IV/IO or 10 mg IM. • 8 Bleeding / Rupture / Abruptio: Place patient in left lateral recumbent position. • • 9 Establish IV access and administer 0.9% Normal Saline 20 mL/kg up to 1,000 mL. • 10 Contact Medical Control for guidance, especially for complications, eclampsia seizures, or difficult deliveries. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ Pre-eclampsia and eclampsia can occur up to 8 weeks AFTER delivery. Always ask about recent pregnancies in female patients presenting with seizures. • ⚠ The primary goal of Magnesium Sulfate administration in eclampsia is to limit and stop seizures, not strictly to lower blood pressure. • ⚠ Maintain a high index of suspicion for uterine rupture in pregnant patients who have experienced trauma, even if minor. • ⚠ Do not pull on the umbilical cord or infant during delivery.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: This protocol outlines the identification and management of sepsis, focusing on the early recognition of a suspected infection combined with abnormal vital signs, and the timely administration of fluids and vasopressors to maintain adequate perfusion.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Suspected infection
  • Immunocompromised states (e.g., cancer, transplant, HIV)
  • Recent surgery or hospitalization
  • Indwelling medical devices

Temp >100.4°F or <96.8°F AND 2 or more of the following: - Altered Mentation - Bradycardia - SBP/MAP thresholds by age - RR (Respiratory Rate) abnormalities - Capillary Refill > 2 seconds - EtCO2 < 25 mmHg

  • Monitor for fluid overload
  • Congestive Heart Failure (CHF)
  • Pulmonary edema
  • Cardiogenic shock
  • Hypovolemia from other causes

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care • • 2 Obtain IV/IO access and administer 0.9% Normal Saline up to 1 L in 250-500 mL boluses (Adult) or 20 mL/kg (Pediatric) max 60 mL/kg • 3 Maintain MAP > 65 mmHg. Norepinephrine infusion 5-20 mcg/min OR Epinephrine infusion 2-10 mcg/min (Adult) • 4 Maintain age adjusted SBP. Epinephrine 0.1 - 1 mcg/kg/min infusion max 10 mcg/min (Pediatric) • 5 Push dose Epinephrine 5-10 mcg IV/IO every 2-3 mins to maintain MAP until infusion is ready (Adult) 6 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control / Prehospital Sepsis Alert MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ CAUTION: Administer IV fluids carefully in patients with a history of CHF or decreased ejection fraction. Monitor closely for signs of fluid overload and pulmonary edema.
  • ⚠ WARNING: Vasopressor infusions (Norepinephrine and Epinephrine) should ideally be administered via an infusion pump to ensure accurate dosing.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines for managing symptomatic hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia, utilizing oral glucose, IV dextrose, IM glucagon, and fluid resuscitation.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Diabetic history
  • Altered mental status
  • Symptomatic hyperglycemia (blood glucose greater than 300 mg/dL)
  • Symptomatic hypoglycemia (blood glucose less than 60 mg/dL)
  • Newborn hypoglycemia (less than 72 hours, blood glucose less than 40 mg/dL)
  • Ability to swallow/protect airway for oral glucose

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol / Check blood glucose • • 2 Hypoglycemia (Conscious, able to swallow):Administer Oral glucose 1 tube PO (Adult & Ped > 1 year).Repeat dose in 5 minutes if no response. • • 3 Hypoglycemia (IV/IO access available):Administer ADULT: Dextrose 10% 125 mL (12.5 g) IV/IOPEDIATRIC: Dextrose 10% 5 mL/kg IV/IO. Maximum dose 125 mL (12.5 g).Repeat dose in 5 minutes if no response. • 4 Hypoglycemia (No IV/IO access):Administer ADULT: Glucagon 1 mg IMPEDIATRIC (< 25 kg): Glucagon 0.2 mg/kg IM. Maximum dose 1 mg.Repeat dose in 10 minutes if no response. • 5 Symptomatic Hyperglycemia:Consider ADULT: 0.9% Sodium chloride up to 1 L IV/IO in 250 – 500 mL bolusesPEDIATRIC: 0.9% Sodium chloride 20 mL/kg IV/IO.Repeat as needed to maintain adequate perfusion, up to 60 mL/kg total. •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Ensure patient can swallow and protect airway before administering oral glucose.
  2. Dextrose 10% is preferred over D50 to reduce tissue necrosis risk if extravasation occurs.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines for managing witnessed or reported seizures, focusing on airway protection, preventing injury, and pharmacological intervention with benzodiazepines.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Witnessed/reported seizure
  • Trauma
  • Overdose
  • Pregnancy
  • Active seizing
  • Altered mental status
  • Airway compromise
  • Exclude Eclampsia if pregnant >20 wks / <8 wks post-partum
  • Hypoglycemia

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Prevent injury (DO NOT restrain while seizing) • • 2 Provide supplemental O2/protect airway • • 3 Assess blood glucose (Treat per protocol) • • 4 Refer to Overdose/Poisoning protocol if indicated • • 5 Actively seizing >5 mins:Midazolam 5 mg IV/IO or 10 mg IM (Adult & Ped >40kg)| Midazolam 2.5 mg IV/IO or 5 mg IM (Ped <40kg)(Repeat once in 5 mins) • 6 Use HALF dose Midazolam for frail/hypotensive/elderly >65y • 7 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control and initiate transport if seizing >5 mins after 2nd dose of Midazolam MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ Both seizures and benzodiazepines cause significant respiratory depression - monitor airway closely. • ⚠ Status epilepticus is a medical emergency requiring rapid treatment and transport. • Pediatric febrile seizures require ED evaluation.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines for the rapid assessment and transport of patients with suspected Stroke/TIA, utilizing the BEFAST assessment and RACE scale to determine appropriate destination routing for LVOs.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Time last seen normal
  • Anticoagulant use
  • Recent surgery/trauma
  • BEFAST: Balance, Eyes, Face, Arms, Speech, Time
  • Altered mental status
  • Calculate RACE scale (0-9)
  • Blood Glucose <60

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Universal Care / Assess BEFAST • • 2 Assess RACE scale for Large Vessel Occlusion (LVO) • • 3 Record Time Last Seen Normal and family contact info • • 4 Assess blood glucose (Treat <60 per Hypoglycemia protocol) • • 5 Goal scene time <15 minutes / Initiate rapid transport • • 6 Triage: Symptoms <24 hrs & RACE 5-9 -> Transport to closest Comprehensive Stroke Center (if <30 min delay) • • 7 Triage: Symptoms >24 hrs OR RACE 0-4 -> Transport to closest Primary Stroke Center • • 8 Establish 18g IV in Right AC (preferred for rapid imaging) • 9 Call Poquoson COR at receiving facility with Name/DOB for rapid registration • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ Immediate life-threats (uncontrolled airway/arrest) go to the nearest ED. • ⚠ Bring medications or medication list with patient. • ⚠ Be prepared to answer ED questions regarding anticoagulants/bleeding disorders. • RACE ≥ 5 indicates high probability of LVO requiring endovascular therapy.

docs.title: Adenosine
docs.text: Trade Name: Adenocard

docs.title: Classification & Action
docs.text:
• Drug Class: Antidysrhythmic
  • Mechanism of Action: Decreases conduction through the AV node

docs.title: Indications
docs.text: • Tachyarrhythmia – Stable Supraventricular Tachycardia (SVT)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
• Second or third degree AV block
  • Wolf-Parkinson White Syndrome (WPW)
  • Sick sinus syndrome
  • Known hypersensitivity
  • Use of Dipyridamole products including Persantine and Aggrenox

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Extremely short half-life – use proximal IV site (eg. Antecubital)
  • May precipitate AV block or lethal arrhythmia (asystole, VF, VT)

docs.title: Adults
docs.text: • 6 mg rapid IV push — followed immediately by a rapid flush. May repeat with 12 mg if no conversion.

docs.title: Pediatrics
docs.text: • 0.1 mg/kg rapid IV push — (max 6 mg first dose) followed immediately by a rapid flush.

docs.title: Pharmacokinetics
docs.text: Onset Duration Seconds 1 - 2 minutes

docs.title: Side Effects
docs.text:
• Brief facial flushing
  • Chest discomfort
  • Bradycardia

docs.title: Interactions
docs.text:
• Methylxanthines (e.g., caffeine, theophylline) antagonize effects.
  • Dipyridamole (Persantine) and Carbamazepine potentiate effects.

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ Warning: May precipitate AV block or lethal arrhythmia (asystole, VF, VT).
  • Extremely short half-life – must use a proximal IV site (e.g., Antecubital) and follow immediately with a rapid saline flush.

docs.title: Albuterol Sulfate
docs.text: Trade Name: Proventil

docs.title: Classification & Action
docs.text:
• Drug Class: Inhaled Beta agonist
  • Mechanism of Action: Bronchodilator, Decreases serum potassium

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:
• Allergic reaction
  • Crush injury
  • Respiratory Distress

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text: • Hypersensitivity/allergy

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text: • May cause tachycardia. Monitor closely, especially when used in combination with other sympathomimetic agents such as Epinephrine.

docs.title: Adults
docs.text: • 2.5 mg in 3 mL Nebulized — May repeat as needed for respiratory distress.

docs.title: Pediatrics
docs.text: • 2.5 mg in 3 mL Nebulized — May repeat as needed.

docs.title: Pharmacokinetics
docs.text: Onset Duration 5 - 15 minutes 3 - 4 hours

docs.title: Side Effects
docs.text:
• Tremor, nervousness
  • Headache, dizziness
  • Hypertension
  • Tachycardia
  • Nausea

docs.title: Interactions
docs.text:
• Beta-blockers may antagonize effects.
  • Sympathomimetics (such as Epinephrine) may exacerbate cardiovascular side effects.

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ Warning: Monitor closely for tachycardia, especially when used in combination with other sympathomimetic agents such as Epinephrine.
  • May be used in crush injuries to assist with decreasing serum potassium.

docs.title: Amiodarone
docs.text: Trade Name: Cordarone

docs.title: Classification & Action
docs.text:
• Drug Class: Antidysrhythmic
  • Mechanism of Action: Sodium and potassium channel blocker. Prolongs cardiac action potential and repolarization time.

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:
• Cardiac Arrest: VT/VF refractory to defibrillation
  • Wide Complex Tachycardia (VT with a pulse)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
• 2nd or 3rd degree AV block
  • Known hypersensitivity

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text: • Caution in use with patients taking Beta blockers, Calcium channel blockers

docs.title: Adults
docs.text:
• 300 mg IV/IO slow push — for Cardiac Arrest (may repeat 150 mg).
  • 150 mg IV/IO over 10 minutes — for Wide Complex Tachycardia with a pulse.

docs.title: Pediatrics
docs.text: • 5 mg/kg IV/IO — for Cardiac Arrest (max 300 mg).

docs.title: Pharmacokinetics
docs.text: Onset Duration Immediate Variable (days to weeks)

docs.title: Side Effects
docs.text:
• Hypotension
  • Bradycardia
  • Nausea/vomiting
  • Flushing

docs.title: Interactions
docs.text: • Beta blockers and Calcium channel blockers may potentiate bradycardia and hypotension.

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ Warning: Caution in use with patients taking Beta blockers or Calcium channel blockers.
  • May cause profound hypotension or bradycardia, particularly if given too rapidly to a patient with a pulse.

docs.title: Epinephrine
docs.text: Trade Name: Adrenaline

docs.title: Classification & Action
docs.text:
• Drug Class: Vasopressor, Sympathomimetic, Catecholamine
  • Mechanism of Action: Increases cardiac contractility. Increases heart rate. Increases peripheral vascular resistance. Bronchodilation. Reduces bronchial edema.

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:

  • Cardiac arrest
  • Respiratory distress & Stridor

  • May cause tissue ischemia / necrosis with extravasation. Use proximal IV site (Antecubital or higher) and verify patency prior to IV administration/infusion.

docs.title: Fentanyl
docs.text: Trade Name: Sublimaze

docs.title: Classification & Action
docs.text:
• Drug Class: Synthetic opiate analgesic
  • Mechanism of Action: Analgesia and sedation

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:
• Pain control
  • Post-advanced airway analgesia/sedation

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
• Systolic BP < 100
  • Hypersensitivity/allergy

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Use lower doses (0.5 mcg/kg or less) and longer spacing between doses in frail, hypotensive, underweight, opiate naïve and elderly patients (> 65 years).
  • Due to the risk of apnea and respiratory arrest, use in combination with benzodiazepines only after an airway has been definitively secured and ability to ventilate adequately has been confirmed.
  • Rare cases of muscle/chest wall rigidity have been reported. Use lowest dose necessary and administer slowly.

docs.title: Classification & Action
docs.text:
• Drug Class: Antipsychotic

docs.text: • Behavioral emergencies: Psychiatric, medical, or traumatic emergencies with behavior which poses a significant risk of harm to the patient or others

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
• Known allergy/hypersensitivity
  • Parkinson’s disease
  • Dementia (including Alzheimer’s disease)
  • CNS depression/Coma

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Many drug-drug interactions, particularly with medications known to prolong the QT interval
  • May cause hypotension. Use with caution in patients with cardiac disease, ≥65 years old, or taking anti-arrhythmic medications.
  • May lower seizure threshold – use only when benefit outweighs risk in patients with known seizure disorder.
  • May cause dystonic reactions (abnormal involuntary movements) or muscle rigidity.
  • May block the activity of epinephrine – use Norepinephrine if vasopressor is required.

docs.title: Classification & Action
docs.text:
• Drug Class: Dissociative anesthetic
  • Mechanism of Action: Analgesic, Dissociative / amnestic (sedation, induction of anesthesia)

docs.text:

  • Sudden death following administration for behavioral emergencies has been reported. Close monitoring, including cardiac monitor, HR, blood pressure, and SpO2 should be initiated as soon as feasible
  • Laryngospasm or apnea may occur, particularly with rapid IV push – administer slowly (over 1-2 minutes) and monitor airway closely following any administration
  • May increase the risk of post-intubation hypotension

  • Paradoxical reactions (agitated/aggressive behavior) have been reported.
  • Half-life may be prolonged in patients with renal impairment.
