docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Respiratory - Airway / Inhalation Irritants
docs.text: OVERVIEW: This protocol addresses exposure to choking agents and irritant gases causing airway injury. Management focuses on: 1) Reduce exposure/rescue and decontamination, 2) Optimize airway/ventilation, 3) Administer treatments/antidotes, 4) Support cardiovascular system.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Exposure to choking agents:   – Chlorine   – Ammonia   – Phosgene   – Fuming Sulfuric Acid   – Hydrogen Sulfide   – Hydrochloric Acid   – Hydrofluoric Acid
  • Duration of exposure
  • Enclosed vs. open space
  • Occupational exposure history
  • Singed facial or nasal hairs
  • Burns or soot on face, nose, or mouth
  • Hoarse voice or stridor
  • Carbonaceous sputum
  • Difficulty breathing/respiratory distress
  • Hypoxia, Tachypnea
  • Throat pain/burning, wheezing
  • Laryngospasm
  • Non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema
  • Chemical pneumonitis
  • Reactive airways dysfunction
  • Delayed pulmonary edema (phosgene)
  • Upper airway obstruction
  • Lower airway bronchospasm
  • Systemic toxicity (hydrogen sulfide)
  • Co-existing thermal burns
  • Concomitant trauma

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Scene Safety / PPE / Initial Assessment:• Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol.• Decision to enter contaminated area rests with incident commander and organizational policy. • • 2 Decontamination (Gross):• Victims must be decontaminated and/or confirmed "clean" prior to treatment and transport.• Remove contaminated clothing.• Brush off dry chemicals before irrigation. • • 3 Airway Assessment:• Assess for signs of airway injury (singed hairs, facial burns, hoarseness, stridor, soot).• Monitor for progressive respiratory distress. • • 4 Oxygen Administration:• Apply high-flow oxygen via NRB or BVM as indicated.• Target SpO₂ > 94%. • • 5 Airway Management - Basic:• Follow Airway Management and Ventilatory Support procedure.• Position patient upright if tolerated. • • 6 Albuterol Sulfate (Bronchodilator):• Adult: 3 mL of 0.083% solution, nebulized; repeat as necessary• Pediatric: 3 mL of 0.083% solution, nebulized; repeat as necessary • 7 Methylprednisolone (Solu-Medrol):• Adult: 125 mg IV/IO slow push• Pediatric: 2 mg/kg IV/IO slow push (Maximum dose: 125 mg) • 8 Sodium Bicarbonate - Chlorine Gas Exposure Only:• Adult: 2 mL diluted with 2 mL 0.9% Sodium chloride, nebulized (ONCE only)• Pediatric: 2 mL diluted with 2 mL 0.9% Sodium chloride, nebulized (ONCE only) • 9 Advanced Airway Assessment:• Consider endotracheal intubation if signs of airway injury develop.• Assess for impending airway compromise (worsening stridor, respiratory failure). • 10 Endotracheal Intubation:• Follow Endotracheal Intubation procedure.• ⚠ Intubation of spontaneously breathing patients without rapid sequence induction (RSI) is generally not recommended. • 11 Poison Control Consultation:• Contact Poison Control Center at 1-800-222-1222 with any questions.(Note: Poison Control is not online medical control.) • 12 Medical Control Contact:• Contact Poquoson OMD for guidance as needed.• Report agent exposure, interventions performed, and patient status. MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Decontamination Safety: Victims must be decontaminated and/or confirmed "clean" prior to treatment and transport to prevent secondary contamination of EMS personnel and equipment.

  2. ⚠ Sodium Bicarbonate - Once Only: For chlorine gas exposure, Sodium bicarbonate nebulizer is administered ONCE only (2 mL diluted with 2 mL 0.9% Sodium chloride). Repeat dosing is not indicated.

  3. ⚠ RSI Contraindication: Intubation of spontaneously breathing patients without rapid sequence induction (RSI) is generally not recommended. RSI is not addressed in Poquoson Protocols—refer to agency specific protocols.

  4. ⚠ Pediatric Intubation: Intubation of children under 12 years old is a Paramedic "red dot" skill only requiring specialized training and authorization.

  5. Delayed Symptoms: Phosgene exposure may cause minimal initial symptoms with delayed onset of pulmonary edema (up to 24 hours). All phosgene exposures require transport and monitoring.

  6. Hydrogen Sulfide Toxicity: Hydrogen sulfide causes cellular asphyxia by inhibiting cytochrome oxidase. Severe exposures may present with "knockdown" (sudden collapse). Rescuers must use SCBA.

  7. Ammonia vs. Chlorine: Ammonia causes upper airway and ocular irritation primarily. Chlorine penetrates deeper causing bronchospasm and alveolar injury. Both may cause laryngospasm.

  8. Chemical Pneumonitis: Chemical pneumonitis from irritant gases can progress to acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). Early aggressive airway management and transport to appropriate facility are critical.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Respiratory - Asthma, COPD, and Reactive Airway Disease
docs.text: OVERVIEW: This protocol addresses acute bronchospasm associated with asthma, COPD, and reactive airway disease. The primary goal is to rapidly reverse bronchoconstriction through inhaled beta-agonists and anticholinergics, with escalation to systemic corticosteroids for moderate to severe symptoms and magnesium sulfate for severe, life-threatening presentations. Prompt recognition of respiratory failure and appropriate intervention by all provider levels is essential.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • History of asthma, COPD, or reactive airway disease
  • Known triggers (allergens, exercise, infections)
  • Recent upper respiratory infection
  • Medication compliance and recent inhaler use
  • Prior intubations or ICU admissions for respiratory distress
  • Moderate Symptoms: Wheezing, mild respiratory distress, speaks in full sentences
  • Moderate to Severe: Increased work of breathing, accessory muscle use, speaks in phrases
  • Severe: Silent chest, tripod positioning, single-word speech, altered mental status
  • Respiratory rate > 30, heart rate > 120
  • Pulse oximetry < 90% on room air
  • Pneumonia
  • Pulmonary Edema / CHF
  • Pneumothorax
  • Anaphylaxis / Allergic Reaction (see Allergic Reaction Protocol)
  • Upper Airway Obstruction / Stridor (see Stridor below)
  • Pulmonary Embolism

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Universal Patient Care / Assessment:• Assess airway, breathing, circulation, and mental status.• Apply pulse oximetry and cardiac monitoring.• Assess severity: Moderate, Moderate to Severe, or Severe. • • 2 Oxygen Administration:• Administer oxygen to maintain SpO₂ ≥ 94%.• Use nasal cannula, simple mask, or non-rebreather as appropriate. • • 3 Albuterol Sulfate (Beta-Agonist):• Adult & Pediatric: 3 mL of 0.083% solution via nebulizer.• Repeat as necessary based on clinical response.• May be administered continuously in severe cases per Poquoson OMD direction. • • 4 Ipratropium Bromide (Anticholinergic):• Adult & Pediatric: 3 mL of 0.02% solution via nebulizer.• ⚠ ONCE ONLY – Do NOT repeat without Poquoson OMD consultation. • • 5 Methylprednisolone (Solu-Medrol) for Moderate to Severe Symptoms:• Adult: 125 mg IV/IO slow push.• Pediatric: 2 mg/kg IV/IO slow push (maximum dose 125 mg).• Administer if wheezing with moderate to severe respiratory distress. • 6 Magnesium Sulfate for Severe Symptoms / Impending Respiratory Failure:• Adult: 2 g IV/IO infusion over 15 minutes.• Pediatric: 40 mg/kg IV/IO over 15 minutes (maximum dose 2,000 mg or 2 g).• Administer if severe bronchospasm unresponsive to initial therapy. • 7 Assist Ventilations / Airway Management:• Prepare for assisted ventilation if respiratory failure develops.• Bag-valve-mask with PEEP valve as indicated.• Consider supraglottic airway or endotracheal intubation if unable to maintain adequate ventilation. • • 8 Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP):• Consider for COPD exacerbation or severe bronchospasm with adequate mentation.• Initiate 5-10 cm H₂O, titrate to effect.• Contraindicated if altered mental status, vomiting, or respiratory arrest imminent. • 9 Related Protocol – Stridor (Upper Airway Obstruction):• Epinephrine: 3 mg [1 mg/mL solution] nebulized.• May be repeated once if needed.• Adult AND Pediatric same dosing.• For anaphylaxis with stridor, follow Allergic Reaction Protocol. • • 10 Methylprednisolone for Moderate to Severe Stridor:• Same dosing as above (Row 5).• Administer after epinephrine nebulization for persistent symptoms. • 11 Reassessment and Transport:• Continuously monitor respiratory status, auscultation, and SpO₂.• Reassess after each bronchodilator treatment.• Expedite transport for severe or deteriorating patients. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Ipratropium Bromide is ONCE ONLY. Do not administer repeat doses without consulting Poquoson OMD. Excessive anticholinergic dosing may worsen outcomes.

  2. Albuterol is Repeat-As-Necessary. EMTs may administer multiple treatments based on clinical response. Continuous nebulized albuterol may be initiated for severe cases with Poquoson OMD direction.

  3. ⚠ Route Selection Matters. Nebulized medications deliver drug directly to airways for bronchospasm. Reserve IV/IO bronchodilators for patients unable to tolerate nebulizer or in impending respiratory failure.

  4. Pediatric Maximum Doses:• Methylprednisolone: 125 mg maximum (regardless of weight-based calculation).• Magnesium Sulfate: 2,000 mg (2 g) maximum (regardless of weight-based calculation).

  5. ⚠ Stridor vs Wheezing: Stridor indicates upper airway obstruction (epiglottitis, croup, anaphylaxis) and responds to nebulized epinephrine. Wheezing indicates lower airway bronchospasm (asthma/COPD) and responds to nebulized albuterol/ipratropium.

  6. Severe Asthma Indicators: Silent chest (no audible wheeze), tripod positioning, inability to speak in phrases, altered mental status, or SpO₂ < 90% despite supplemental oxygen warrant immediate escalation to magnesium sulfate and preparation for airway support.

  7. Steroids Take Time. Methylprednisolone onset is 1-2 hours; do not delay transport waiting for clinical improvement. Early administration improves outcomes but is not immediately life-saving in acute crisis.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Respiratory - Pediatric Stridor
docs.text: OVERVIEW: Stridor in pediatric patients indicates upper airway obstruction requiring rapid assessment and intervention. The priority is maintaining a calm environment as agitation worsens obstruction. Nebulized epinephrine and corticosteroids are primary treatments with airway protection as the ultimate priority.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Recent upper respiratory illness (croup, epiglottitis)
  • Choking episode or foreign body aspiration
  • Congenital airway anomalies
  • Sudden onset vs. gradual progression
  • Vaccination status (H. influenzae type b)
  • Onset with fever or drooling
  • Stridor (inspiratory > expiratory)
  • Hoarse voice or barking seal cough
  • Difficulty breathing / retractions
  • Tripod positioning
  • Drooling / inability to swallow
  • Agitation or altered mental status
  • Cyanosis
  • Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis):
  • Gradual onset, barking cough
  • Low-grade fever, inspiratory stridor
  • Epiglottitis:
  • Sudden onset, toxic appearance
  • High fever, tripod position
  • No cough, severe drooling
  • Foreign body aspiration
  • Bacterial tracheitis
  • Retropharyngeal abscess

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact• Follow Universal Patient Care Poquoson Protocol for all patients. • • 2 Scene Management & Calm Environment• Keep child calm - agitation worsens obstruction• Allow parent/caregiver to hold child• Avoid unnecessary procedures• Minimize stimulation and stranger anxiety • • 3 Position of Comfort• Allow child to assume position of choice (usually upright or with parent)• Do not force supine positioning• Head-down positioning for infants <1 year if foreign body suspected • • 4 High-Flow Oxygen• Apply high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask or blow-by• Monitor SpO2 continuously• Titrate to maintain SpO2 >94% • • 5 Continuous Monitoring• Cardiac monitor• Pulse oximetry• EtCO2 when available • • 6 CPAP for Upper Airway Support• Consider CPAP for upper airway support if indicated• Initial PEEP 5 cmH2O (titratable by I/P providers) • 7 Albuterol Nebulizer• Albuterol 3 mL 0.083% solution nebulized• May help with lower airway component• Reassess after treatment • 8 Systemic Corticosteroids• Methylprednisolone (Solu-Medrol) per dosing table below:   • Adult: 125 mg IV/IO slow push   • Pediatric: 2 mg/kg IV/IO slow push (max 125 mg)• Steroids take 30-60 minutes to take effect • 9 IV/IO Access• Obtain IV or IO access for medication administration• Use largest appropriate catheter• Do NOT delay epinephrine for IV/IO placement • 10 Nebulized Epinephrine for Moderate to Severe Stridor• Epinephrine 3 mg [1 mg/mL] nebulized• May be repeated once if needed• Same concentration as adults and pediatrics 11 Airway Protection - Advanced Airway• Prepare for advanced airway if impending respiratory failure• Have appropriate sized equipment ready• ⚠ Intubation of children <12 requires Paramedic skill MC 12 Transport Considerations• Transport to pediatric-capable facility• Alert receiving facility early• Consider ALS intercept if BLS only• Rapid transport if severe obstruction • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Critical Intubation Warning: Intubation of children under 12 years of age is a Paramedic "red dot" skill hazard only. Intermediate providers must contact Medical Control prior to attempting intubation in pediatric patients.

  2. Keep Child Calm: Agitation, crying, and fighting increase oxygen demand and worsen upper airway obstruction. Allow the child to remain in the parent's arms and avoid unnecessary stimulation.

  3. Parental Presence is Critical: Parental presence and reassurance are therapeutic interventions. Never separate a distressed child from their caregiver unless absolutely necessary for medical intervention.

  4. Steroid Onset Time: Methylprednisolone and other corticosteroids require 30-60 minutes to demonstrate clinical effect. Nebulized epinephrine provides rapid, temporary relief while steroids take effect.

  5. Croup vs. Epiglottitis Differentiation:

  6. Croup: Gradual onset, barking cough, low-grade fever, responsive to nebulized epinephrine

  7. Epiglottitis: Sudden onset, severe toxicity, high fever, tripod positioning, no cough, severe drooling - requires immediate advanced airway readiness

  8. Nebulized Epinephrine Specifics:

  9. Use 3 mg of 1 mg/mL epinephrine solution (equivalent to 3 mL)
  10. Can be repeated once if no improvement

  11. Rebound stridor may occur after 1-2 hours - ensure appropriate transport and monitoring

  12. Do Not Examine the Oropharynx in Epiglottitis: Visualizing the oropharynx may provoke laryngospasm and complete airway obstruction. If epiglottitis is suspected, avoid any unnecessary airway manipulation and prepare for immediate advanced airway management.

  13. Foreign Body Airway Obstruction: If foreign body is suspected, follow appropriate choking protocol for age group. Blind finger sweeps are strictly prohibited - use direct visualization and Magill forceps only if trained.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Respiratory - Pneumothorax / Tension Pneumothorax
docs.text: OVERVIEW: Tension pneumothorax is a life-threatening condition where air accumulates in the pleural space under pressure, causing mediastinal shift, cardiovascular collapse, and respiratory failure. Immediate needle decompression is required to convert the tension pneumothorax to a simple pneumothorax, relieving the life-threatening pressure and restoring hemodynamic stability.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Traumatic or spontaneous mechanism
  • Penetrating or blunt chest trauma
  • Recent invasive procedure (central line, thoracentesis)
  • COPD or asthma exacerbation
  • Mechanical ventilation
  • Respiratory distress
  • Hemodynamic instability (hypotension, tachycardia)
  • Absent or diminished breath sounds on affected side
  • Tracheal deviation (late sign)
  • Jugular venous distension (JVD)
  • Tachypnea, hypoxia
  • Cyanosis
  • Traumatic cardiac arrest with signs/suspicion of thoracoabdominal trauma
  • Tension Pneumothorax
  • Simple Pneumothorax
  • Hemothorax
  • Pulmonary Contusion
  • Cardiac Tamponade
  • Flail Chest
  • Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)
  • Airway Obstruction

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Universal Patient Care / Scene Safety:• Ensure scene safety and appropriate PPE.• Bring all necessary equipment to patient side. • • 2 Primary Assessment:• Assess airway, breathing, circulation, and disability.• Assess vital signs and SpO2.• Identify life threats immediately. • • 3 Oxygen Administration:• Administer Oxygen via nasal cannula or mask as needed to maintain SpO2 > 94%.• Titrate to patient condition. • • 4 Positioning and Monitoring:• Position patient for comfort (typically supine or semi-Fowler's).• Establish IV/IO access.• Continuous cardiac and pulse oximetry monitoring. • • 5 Suction and Airway Management:• Prepare suction and airway equipment.• Be prepared for rapid airway intervention if decompensation occurs. • • 6 Needle Decompression - Site Selection:• Locate 2nd intercostal space in mid-clavicular line on affected side.• Alternate site: 4th intercostal space, mid-axillary line. • 7 Needle Decompression - Equipment:• Attach 10 mL 0.9% Sodium Chloride syringe to 14-gauge catheter needle.• Use longest available needle (3.25 inch preferred for adult/trauma patients). • 8 Needle Decompression - Procedure:• Aspirate syringe while advancing over the superior aspect of the rib.• Insert perpendicular to the skin into the pleural space.• Continue until "pop" felt or air/blood/fluid freely aspirated.• ⚠ Do NOT advance needle further.• Advance catheter over needle into pleural space.• Remove needle, leave catheter in place. • 9 Bilateral Chest Decompression (Traumatic Cardiac Arrest):• For traumatic cardiac arrest with suspicion of thoracoabdominal trauma, perform bilateral needle decompression. • 10 Reassessment After Decompression:• Continuously reassess vital signs, respiratory effort, and compliance.• Auscultate for return of breath sounds.• Monitor for hemodynamic improvement. • • 11 Repeat Decompression if Needed:• Consider repeating procedure for continued or recurrent hemodynamic instability or absent breath sounds. • 12 Hemodynamic Support:• 0.9% Sodium Chloride fluid bolus if hypotensive (SBP < 90 mmHg or MAP < 65).• Consider vasopressor support if cardiogenic shock present.• Titrate fluids to perfusion. • 13 Advanced Airway Management (if indicated):• Prepare for or perform endotracheal intubation if respiratory failure.• ⚠ Positive pressure ventilation (BVM/CPAP) may worsen or induce pneumothorax. • 14 Transport Considerations:• Transport to appropriate trauma center if traumatic etiology.• Alert receiving facility of needle decompression performed.• Consider air medical transport for severe cases. • • 15 Close Monitoring En Route:• Continuous reassessment en route.• Monitor for recurrent tension pneumothorax.• Secure catheter to prevent dislodgement. • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ CPAP is contraindicated in patients with pneumothorax. Positive pressure ventilation may worsen the condition or cause tension physiology.
  2. ⚠ Positive pressure ventilation (BVM/CPAP) may worsen or induce pneumothorax. Use caution when ventilating patients with suspected pneumothorax.
  3. ⚠ Bilateral Chest Decompression in Traumatic Cardiac Arrest: For traumatic cardiac arrest with signs/suspicion of thoracoabdominal trauma, perform bilateral chest decompression to address potential bilateral tension pneumothorax.
  4. Equipment Selection: Use the longest available needle (3.25 inch preferred) for adequate depth to reach the pleural space, especially in obese or muscular patients. Standard 1.5-inch needles may be insufficient.
  5. ⚠ Needle Advancement Safety: Once the "pop" is felt or air/blood/fluid is freely aspirated, do NOT advance the needle further to avoid injury to underlying structures (lung, heart, great vessels).
  6. Anatomical Landmarks: Primary site is 2nd intercostal space at mid-clavicular line. Alternate acceptable position is 4th intercostal space at mid-axillary line (preferred in some obese patients or when mid-clavicular approach is suboptimal).
  7. Reassessment for Recurrence: Tension pneumothorax can recur even after successful decompression. Continuously reassess vital signs, respiratory effort, and compliance. If deterioration occurs, repeat the procedure immediately.
  8. ⚠ Breath Stacking in Obstructive Airway Disease: If breath stacking occurs in patients with obstructive airway disease, disconnect the BVM and allow the patient to exhale.
  9. Hemodynamic Support: No specific medications treat pneumothorax itself. Support is directed at correcting hypovolemia/shock with fluids and managing associated conditions.
  10. Catheter Patency: The catheter may become occluded with blood or tissue. Monitor for reaccumulation of tension and be prepared to place additional catheters if needed.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Respiratory - Pulmonary Edema and Congestion
docs.text: Management of acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema and congestion due to congestive heart failure, infection, or near-drowning. Requires immediate respiratory support, afterload reduction, and diuresis when appropriate.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Congestive heart failure (CHF)
  • Recent infection
  • Near-drowning event
  • Cardiac history / Myocardial Infarction
  • Respiratory distress (accessory muscle use, retractions)
  • SpO2 < 94%
  • Respiratory rate > 24
  • Inability to speak in complete sentences
  • Pulmonary edema (crackles/rales)
  • Peripheral edema
  • Jugular venous distension (JVD)
  • Productive cough
  • Asthma / COPD Exacerbation
  • Pneumonia / Pulmonary infection
  • Pulmonary Embolism
  • Myocardial Infarction / ACS
  • Anaphylaxis

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol • • 2 Perform focused evaluation including assessment of airway and lung sounds • • 3 Provide supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 > 94% • • 4 Consider non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (CPAP) for severe respiratory distress • • 5 CPAP Initial Settings: PEEP 5 cmH2O • • 6 CPAP CONTRAINDICATIONS (⚠️):• Apnea/agonal respirations• GCS ≤ 13• Pneumothorax • • 7 CPAP CONTRAINDICATIONS (⚠️):• Facial trauma/burns/recent surgery• Penetrating neck/chest trauma• Active vomiting/severe nausea • • 8 CPAP CONTRAINDICATIONS (⚠️):• Hypotension (SBP < 90 mmHg, MAP < 65)• Inability to tolerate tight fitting mask • • 9 CPAP Titration: Adjust PEEP up to 10 cmH2O to support ventilation and work of breathing • 10 NitroglycerinAdult: 0.4 mg SL • 11 Nitroglycerin Repeat Administration:May repeat every 5 minutes as needed if SBP remains > 100 mmHg / MAP > 65 mmHg (maximum 3 doses) • 12 Furosemide (Lasix)Adult: 40 mg IV/IO slow push • 13 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for guidance as needed MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. Early use of non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (CPAP) has been shown to reduce the need for intubation and improve outcomes.
  2. Positive pressure ventilation does not replace the need for treatment of the underlying cause.
  3. ⚠️ Close monitoring for the development of hypotension and/or pneumothorax is required when utilizing CPAP.
  4. ⚠️ Use caution when administering Epinephrine to patients with suspected reactive airway disease who also have signs of congestive heart failure.
  5. ⚠️ Exercise cautious diuresis (Furosemide) in patients to prevent acute hypotension or worsening of shock states.
  6. Patients with a history of CHF may present with wheezing ("cardiac asthma"); properly differentiate from true bronchospasm.
  7. If patient becomes apneic, agonal, or GCS drops below 14 while on CPAP, immediately remove mask and begin BVM ventilation.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Respiratory - Respiratory Arrest / Airway Management
docs.text: OVERVIEW: Respiratory arrest (apnea) and respiratory failure represent life-threatening emergencies requiring immediate airway management and ventilatory support. This protocol provides a systematic approach to securing and maintaining a patent airway, from basic adjuncts through advanced airway techniques, with emphasis on continuous monitoring and prevention of complications such as hypoxia and hyperventilation.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Respiratory failure or arrest (apnea)
  • Inability to maintain patent airway
  • Altered level of consciousness
  • Witnessed choking or foreign body aspiration
  • Absence of spontaneous respirations
  • Inadequate or ineffective breathing
  • Altered mental status
  • SpO2 desaturation
  • Absent breath sounds
  • Airway obstruction (foreign body, trauma, edema)
  • Drug overdose or toxicity
  • Neuromuscular failure
  • Severe metabolic derangement
  • Cardiac arrest with respiratory component

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact:• Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact Poquoson Protocol.• Assess for life threats and mechanism of respiratory failure. • • 2 Position Patient and Open Airway:• Position the patient appropriately (supine for intubation, recovery position if vomiting).• Open the airway using head-tilt chin-lift or jaw-thrust maneuver. • • 3 Continuous Monitoring:• Apply continuous pulse oximetry (SpO2).• Apply continuous box-waveform capnography (EtCO2) once airway placed. • • 4 BLS Airway Adjuncts (EMR/EMT):• Insert oropharyngeal airway (OPA) as indicated.• Insert nasopharyngeal airway (NPA) if gag reflex present.• Use supraglottic airway (SGA) as necessary. • • 5 Suctioning:• Provide suctioning of oropharynx as needed to clear secretions, blood, or vomitus.• Pre-oxygenate before any airway manipulation. • • 6 BVM Ventilation (Two-Person Technique):• Support respirations with bag-valve-mask using two-person technique preferred.• Deliver appropriate tidal volume (6-7 mL/kg) at rate of 10-12 breaths/min (adult).• Avoid over-ventilation. • • 7 EtCO2-Guided Ventilation:• Use in-line EtCO2 monitoring during BVM ventilation.• Adjust tidal volume, respiratory rate, and/or PEEP to maintain EtCO2 35–45 mmHg.• Target 35-45 mmHg once advanced airway placed. • • 8 Endotracheal Intubation – Adult (≥12 years):• If apneic and unable to ventilate adequately by less invasive means, consider endotracheal intubation.• Verify placement with continuous box-waveform capnography and auscultation. • 9 Endotracheal Intubation – Pediatric (<12 years):• ⚠ RED DOT SKILL: Intubation of children <12 years is a Paramedic "red dot" skill only.• If apneic and <12 years old, consider intubation if unable to ventilate adequately by less invasive means. 10 Foreign Body Airway Obstruction:• If foreign body airway obstruction suspected and unable to ventilate, perform laryngoscopy.• Remove foreign body with Magill forceps under direct visualization. • 11 Surgical Airway (Cricothyrotomy):• If unable to intubate and unable to oxygenate, proceed immediately to Surgical Airway.• Use 14G, 3.25" catheter or surgical cricothyrotomy kit per Poquoson Protocols. 12 Post-Intubation Airway Management:• Verify tube placement with continuous box-waveform capnography AND auscultation (bilateral lung sounds, absence of gastric sounds).• Secure tube with tape or commercial tube holder.• Document depth of placement at teeth/gums. • 13 Ongoing Ventilation Monitoring:• Monitor ET tube placement and ventilation continuously with SpO2 and EtCO2.• Reassess lung sounds and gastric sounds after any patient movement. • 14 Post-Advanced Airway Analgesia – Fentanyl (Sublimaze):• ADULT: 25–50 mcg IV/IO over 2 minutes or IN/IM.• PEDIATRIC: 0.5–1 mcg/kg IV/IO over 2 minutes or IN/IM (Maximum dose 25 mcg).• Repeat every 10–15 minutes as needed. 15 Post-Advanced Airway Sedation – Midazolam (Versed):• ADULT: 2.5 mg IV/IO over 2 minutes or 5 mg IN/IM.• PEDIATRIC: 0.1 mg/kg IV/IO over 2 minutes or 0.2 mg/kg IN/IM (Maximum dose 2.5 mg IV/IO or 5 mg IN/IM).• Repeat every 10–15 minutes as needed.

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. EtCO2 Target: Maintain continuous box-waveform EtCO2 at 35–45 mmHg once advanced airway is placed. This is the definitive indicator of adequate ventilation and perfusion.

  2. ⚠ Over-Ventilation Avoidance: Hyperventilation causes cerebral vasoconstriction, reduced coronary perfusion, and gastric insufflation. Deliver only enough tidal volume to produce visible chest rise (6-7 mL/kg).

  3. ⚠ Pediatric Red Dot Skill: Intubation of children <12 years is a Paramedic "red dot" skill only. Intermediate providers must defer to Paramedic or utilize SGA/BVM ventilation for pediatric patients.

  4. Confirmation of Tube Placement: Always verify advanced airway placement with BOTH continuous box-waveform capnography AND auscultation. Absence of EtCO2 waveform indicates esophageal intubation or loss of perfusion.

  5. Two-Person BVM Technique: Whenever possible, use two providers for BVM ventilation—one to seal the mask and open the airway, one to squeeze the bag. This significantly improves tidal volume delivery.

  6. ⚠ Sedation Precautions: Use half doses of Fentanyl and Midazolam for frail, hypotensive, underweight, and/or elderly (>65 years) patients. Monitor closely for respiratory depression.

  7. ⚠ Opioid-Benzodiazepine Combination: Due to risk of apnea, use Fentanyl in combination with Midazolam only after airway is definitively secured (ET tube or SGA in place).

  8. Foreign Body Management: If complete airway obstruction is suspected and BVM ventilation fails, proceed immediately to laryngoscopy with Magill forceps removal or surgical airway if visualization is impossible.

  9. Continuous Reassessment: Re-verify ET tube placement after every patient movement, transfer, or position change. Dislodgement can occur silently—continuous EtCO2 monitoring is essential.

  10. Documentation Requirements: Document method of airway management, size of airway device, depth of ET tube placement, number of intubation attempts, confirmation methods used, and patient response to sedation/analgesia.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Respiratory - Respiratory Distress
docs.text: This protocol addresses respiratory distress from obstructive (asthma/COPD), infectious (pneumonia), edematous (CHF), or traumatic etiologies. Critical objectives include immediate identification of life-threatening conditions (airway obstruction, respiratory failure, tension pneumothorax/hemothorax, cardiac ischemia), initiation of targeted therapy, and maintenance of oxygen saturation above 94%. Continuous EtCO2 monitoring guides therapy and alerts providers to impending respiratory failure.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Respiratory distress (asthma, COPD, pneumonia, CHF, near-drowning, pulmonary embolism, infection)
  • Trauma to chest/neck
  • Allergic reaction history
  • CHF/cardiogenic pulmonary edema
  • Hypoventilation syndromes (obesity, neuromuscular disease)
  • Recent surgery/anesthesia
  • Smoke inhalation
  • Accessory muscle use, retractions, nasal flaring
  • SpO2 <94% on room air
  • Respiratory rate >24/min (adult) or outside pediatric norms
  • Inability to speak in complete sentences
  • Pulmonary/peripheral edema, JVD
  • Productive cough, wheezing, stridor
  • Altered mental status
  • Cyanosis, diaphoresis
  • Asymmetric chest rise
  • Pneumonia/Pneumocystis
  • Acute pulmonary embolism
  • Pleural effusion
  • Cardiac ischemia/MI
  • Anaphylaxis
  • Flail chest/hemothorax
  • Tension pneumothorax
  • Smoke inhalation
  • Bronchiolitis (pediatric)

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact Protocol:• Assess airway patency and breathing adequacy • • 2 Airway and Lung Assessment:• Focused assessment for stridor, wheezing, rales• Auscultate breath sounds bilaterally• Assess work of breathing and accessory muscle use • • 3 Continuous Monitoring:• Continuous EtCO2 monitoring to guide therapy• Continuous pulse oximetry• Cardiac monitoring • • 4 Oxygen Administration (if SpO2 <94%):• Supplemental Oxygen titrated to maintain SpO2 >94%• Start with nasal cannula or non-rebreather as needed• Avoid excessive FiO2 in COPD patients (target 88-92%) • • 5 Positioning:• Patient position of comfort (sitting upright preferred)• Consider reverse Trendelenburg if tolerated • • 6 *CPAP* (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure):• Indications: Adults with respiratory distress due to pulmonary edema, infection, or near-drowning• Initial Settings: PEEP 5 cmH2O• Titration: Up to 10 cmH2O• Close monitoring for hypotension or pneumothorax⚠ See Pearls for all contraindications MC MC 7 Supraglottic Airways (SGA):• Consider if patient continues to deteriorate and cannot tolerate CPAP• Maintains airway patency while avoiding invasive procedures 8 Advanced Airway Management (ETI):• Endotracheal intubation reserved for I-level providers per protocol • 9 IV/IO Access:• Establish vascular access for medication administration and fluid therapy• IO if IV unobtainable • 10 Fluid Administration:• 0.9% Sodium Chloride or appropriate fluid as clinically indicated• Monitor for signs of fluid overload in CHF patients MC MC 11 Stridor Management (Upper Airway):• Epinephrine 3 mg of 1 mg/mL (1:1,000) nebulized• May be repeated once• May need assistance to hold nebulizer• Administer simultaneously with the nebulized epinephrine treatment:   • Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV/IO (adult) or 2 mg/kg IV/IO pediatric (maximum 125 mg)   ⚠ Single dose only MC MC 12 Bronchiolitis (Pediatric ONLY):• 0.9% Sodium Chloride 6 mL nebulized• Repeat as needed for increased work of breathing• Transport in position of comfort with caregiver 13 Pulmonary Edema:• If Dyspnea with crackles and/or jugular venous distention:• Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual (adult only)• Repeat every 5 minutes up to 3 doses if SBP >100 mmHg (MAP >65 mmHg)• Consider CPAP if available and no contraindications 14 Severe Asthma/COPD:• Refer to Asthma specific protocol for comprehensive treatment• Magnesium Sulfate per Asthma protocol⚠ Positive pressure may worsen pneumothorax or cause breath stacking 15 Cardiac Ischemia Management:• Evaluate for cardiac origin• Consider 12-lead ECG• Refer to Chest Pain/ACS protocol if indicated • • 16 Respiratory Failure Management:• If apnea/agonal respirations or GCS ≤13:• Prepare for immediate airway control• Bag-valve-mask ventilation• Consider advanced airway per provider level• Transport without delay •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. Early use of CPAP reduces the need for intubation in appropriate patients with pulmonary edema
  2. ⚠ CPAP CONTRAINDICATIONS: Apnea/agonal respirations; GCS ≤13; Pneumothorax (suspected or confirmed); Facial trauma/burns/recent surgery; Penetrating neck/chest trauma; Active vomiting; Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg); Inability to tolerate mask
  3. ⚠ CPAP is authorized for EMT providers but requires Medical Control contact for A-level providers
  4. ⚠ I/P providers may titrate CPAP PEEP up to 10 cmH2O
  5. ⚠ Breath Stacking Warning: If breath stacking occurs during positive pressure ventilation, disconnect the BVM and allow for exhalation
  6. If patient continues to deteriorate despite CPAP, consider supraglottic airway or prepare for endotracheal intubation
  7. In CHF/pulmonary edema, avoid aggressive fluid resuscitation; consider nitroglycerin (sublingual if SBP permits) before CPAP if experienced provider available
  8. Close monitoring is required during CPAP for hypotension or development of pneumothorax
  9. Pediatric respiratory distress: Consider bronchiolitis in infants with wheezing and respiratory distress; normal saline nebulizer can reduce work of breathing
  10. Maintain continuous EtCO2 monitoring throughout care to detect early signs of respiratory fatigue and guide interventions

docs.title: Mandatory Text Replacements Applied
docs.text:
• Peninsula EMS / PEMS ➔ Poquoson Fire and Rescue
  • PEMS Medical Advisory Committee / Regional Medical Director ➔ Poquoson OMD
  • Regional Protocol(s) ➔ Poquoson Protocol(s)

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Respiratory - Smoke Inhalation
docs.text: OVERVIEW: The management of smoke inhalation involves the assessment and treatment of airway injury from thermal and chemical exposure to combustion products, with particular attention to potential cyanide toxicity from fires in enclosed spaces. Patients exposed to smoke may develop progressive airway edema requiring prompt intervention, and those from enclosed space fires are at high risk for cyanide poisoning necessitating specific antidote therapy.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Fire/explosion in enclosed space
  • Duration of exposure
  • Type of materials burning (synthetics vs. natural)
  • Loss of consciousness at scene
  • Exposure to combustion products

Airway Injury Indicators: - Singed facial or nasal hairs - Burns or soot on face, nose, or mouth - Hoarse voice or stridor - Carbonaceous sputum - Difficulty breathing/respiratory distress - Hypoxia, Tachypnea Cyanide Poisoning: - Altered mental status - Seizures - Hypotension - Cardiac arrest

  • Direct thermal injury to airway
  • Chemical irritation (acid gases, aldehydes)
  • Carbon monoxide toxicity
  • Cyanide toxicity (from combustion of synthetics)
  • Upper airway obstruction secondary to edema
  • Pneumonitis/ARDS development
  • Consider concomitant burn injuries

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact:• Follow Poquoson Protocols for Universal Patient Care• Remove patient from source and ensure scene safety• Initiate PPE appropriate for smoke/hazardous environment • • 2 Scene Safety and Scene Time Considerations:• Assess scene for ongoing hazard exposure• Limit scene time; prepare for rapid transport• Coordinate with fire department/hazmat as needed • • 3 Airway Assessment:• Inspect for singed facial/nasal hairs• Examine face, nose, and mouth for burns or soot• Auscultate for stridor, hoarseness, or voice changes• Assess sputum for carbonaceous material• Monitor respiratory effort and work of breathing • • 4 Initial Airway Management:• Position patient to optimize ventilation• Suction oropharynx as needed for soot/secretions• Apply high-flow oxygen via appropriate delivery device • • 5 Advanced Airway Considerations:• Consider endotracheal intubation versus immediate transport to the nearest emergency department if any signs or symptoms of airway injury develop• Follow Endotracheal Intubation procedure if proceeding• Continuously reassess airway patency during transport • 6 Endotracheal Intubation (If Indicated):• Perform endotracheal intubation per procedure⚠ Intubation of spontaneously breathing patients without RSI is generally not recommended⚠ Intubation of children under 12 years old is a Paramedic "red dot" skill only MC MC 7 Hydroxocobalamin (Cyanide Antidote) - Indication Assessment:• Assess for exposure to combustion products/fire in an enclosed space with ANY of the following:  - Altered mental status  - Seizures  - Hypotension  - Cardiac arrest MC MC 8 Hydroxocobalamin Preparation:• Reconstitute with 200 mL 0.9% Sodium Chloride• Use vented IV tubing for administration• Verify solution clarity and integrity prior to administration 9 Hydroxocobalamin Administration:• Administer per Poquoson OMD guidance• Monitor for increase in blood pressure• Inform receiving facility of administration• Prepare for possible red/purple urine discoloration in patient MC MC 10 Burn Assessment:• Assess for concomitant thermal burn injuries• Estimate total body surface area (TBSA) involved• Evaluate burn depth and critical areas (face, hands, perineum, major joints) • • 11 Fluid Management (If Burns Present):• Initiate fluid resuscitation per Burn Injuries Protocol• Titrate fluid administration to hemodynamic response• Monitor urine output if catheter in place • 12 Medical Control Contact:• Contact Poquoson OMD for cyanide poisoning confirmation and specific Hydroxocobalamin dosing guidance• Contact Regional Burn Center for significant burn injuries requiring specialized care• Coordinate destination for combined airway/burn injuries MC MC 13 Monitoring and Reassessment:• Monitor SpO2, EtCO2, and respiratory rate continuously• Reassess airway patency frequently; edema may progress rapidly• Observe for signs of respiratory failure requiring immediate airway intervention • • 14 Transport Considerations:• Expedited transport given risk of rapid airway compromise• Transport to facility capable of managing combined trauma/burns if indicated• Consider aeromedical evacuation for significant burn centers if prolonged ground transport • •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. Airway Injury Progression: Airway edema from smoke inhalation can progress rapidly over minutes to hours following exposure. Frequent reassessment and a low threshold for advanced airway intervention is essential in patients with any indicators of airway injury.

  2. ⚠ RSI Consideration: Intubation of spontaneously breathing patients without rapid sequence induction (RSI) is generally not recommended. Consider allowing patient to maintain spontaneous ventilation when possible, proceeding to intubation only if respiratory failure develops.

  3. ⚠ Pediatric Intubation: Endotracheal intubation of children under 12 years old is a strictly Paramedic "red dot" skill only. Intermediate providers MUST NOT intubate pediatric patients under 12 years of age.

  4. Hydroxocobalamin Mechanism: Hydroxocobalamin binds cyanide ions to form cyanocobalamin, which is excreted in the urine. This action effectively removes cyanide from cellular cytochrome oxidase, allowing aerobic metabolism to resume.

  5. ⚠ Hydroxocobalamin Side Effects: Administration of Hydroxocobalamin may cause significant increase in blood pressure; use caution in patients with severe hypertension. The medication causes deep red/purple discoloration of urine, skin, and mucous membranes which is expected and may persist for days.

  6. Preparation Critical: Hydroxocobalamin requires reconstitution with 200 mL 0.9% Sodium Chloride and MUST be administered using vented IV tubing. Failure to use proper preparation may result in ineffective delivery.

  7. Burn Center Referral: Contact the Regional Burn Center early for patients with significant burn injuries, especially those involving the airway or extensive total body surface area. Early coordination facilitates optimal definitive care.

  8. Multi-Toxicity Consideration: Patients from enclosed space fires may have combined carbon monoxide, cyanide, and thermal inhalation injuries. High-flow oxygen addresses carbon monoxide; Hydroxocobalamin addresses cyanide. Neither antidote negates the need for airway protection from thermal injury.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Respiratory - Upper Airway Obstruction / Foreign Body Airway Obstruction
Overview
docs.text: Management of acute or impending airway obstruction including expanding hematoma, edema, mass, or foreign body obstruction. Requires rapid recognition and appropriate intervention based on patient consciousness, age, and body habitus. Foreign body airway obstruction (FBAO) is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate mechanical relief.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Acute or impending airway obstruction
  • Expanding hematoma, edema, or mass
  • Witnessed choking episode
  • Sudden onset during eating
  • History of trauma to neck/airway
  • Inability to maintain patent airway
  • Secretions, blood, vomit obstructing airway
  • Complete or partial obstruction
  • Stridor, hoarseness, or voice changes
  • Cyanosis or profound respiratory distress
  • Inability to speak, cough, or breathe
  • Foreign body aspiration
  • Anaphylaxis with angioedema
  • Traumatic airway compromise
  • Infection (epiglottitis, abscess)
  • Tumor or mass obstruction
  • Post-procedural airway edema

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Universal Patient Care:• Apply to all patients per Poquoson Protocols. • • 2 Scene Safety / Recognition:• Rapidly identify complete vs partial obstruction.• Recognize universal choking sign (hands to throat).• Determine conscious vs unconscious status. • • 3 Conscious Adult (≥12 years) - Abdominal Thrusts:• Stand behind patient, wrap arms around waist.• Make fist with one hand; thumb side against patient's abdomen.• Position fist midline, below xiphoid process and above navel.• Grasp fist with other hand and deliver quick, upward thrusts.• Repeat until obstruction clears or patient becomes unconscious. • • 4 Conscious Obese Adult / Pregnant Woman - Chest Thrusts:• Stand behind patient, arms under patient's armpits.• Make fist with one hand; thumb side against sternum.• Position fist midline on lower half of sternum.• Grasp fist with other hand and deliver backward thrusts.• Repeat until obstruction clears or patient becomes unconscious. • • 5 Conscious Pediatric ≥1 year - Abdominal Thrusts:• Kneel behind child, wrap arms around waist.• Make fist; thumb side against child's abdomen.• Position fist midline, below xiphoid process and above navel.• Deliver quick, upward thrusts until obstruction clears. • • 6 Conscious Pediatric <1 year - Back Blows and Chest Thrusts:• Position infant prone, head down, below level of chest.• Support head and neck with hand; jaw supported by thumb and fingers.• Deliver 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades using heel of hand.• Turn infant supine, head down; deliver 5 chest thrusts on lower sternum.• Repeat alternating 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts until obstruction clears. • • 7 Unconscious Patient - Position and Open Airway:• Place patient supine on firm surface.• Open airway using head-tilt chin-lift or jaw-thrust if trauma suspected. • • 8 Unconscious Patient - BLS Airway Adjuncts:• Insert OPA or NPA as indicated by patient tolerance and level of consciousness.• Insert SGA (i-gel or King) if unable to maintain airway with basic maneuvers. • • 9 Suctioning:• Provide oropharyngeal suctioning as needed for secretions, blood, or vomit.• Use rigid Yankauer catheter for direct visualization. • • 10 Apneic Patient - BVM Ventilation (EMT and above):• Support respirations with BVM and appropriate airway adjunct.• Ensure adequate seal and proper ventilation rate.• Monitor SpO2 and EtCO2 continuously. • • 11 Unable to Ventilate - Laryngoscopy and Forceps Removal (Intermediate/Paramedic):• If unable to ventilate due to foreign body obstruction, perform direct laryngoscopy.• Visualize foreign body using laryngoscope blade.• Remove obstruction with Magill forceps under direct visualization.• Do NOT perform blind finger sweeps (may impact object deeper). • 12 Unable to Ventilate - Endotracheal Intubation:• If apneic and unable to ventilate by less invasive means, consider endotracheal intubation.• Visualize cords and pass ET tube under direct visualization.⚠ Intubation of children <12 years is a Paramedic "red dot" skill only. MC 13 Post-Obstruction Advanced Airway - Sedation:• If endotracheal intubation performed, refer to Sedation Post Advanced Airway protocol.• Fentanyl (25 mcg) and Midazolam (2.5 mg) per Respiratory Arrest protocol.• Use half-doses in elderly or frail patients. MC MC 14 Expanding Hematoma / Edema - Advanced Airway:• Early airway intervention before complete obstruction.• Consider difficult airway algorithm; prepare surgical airway backup.• Contact Medical Control for guidance in complex airways. MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. Conscious vs. Unconscious Algorithm:Conscious patients receive mechanical relief (Heimlich/chest thrusts). Unconscious patients receive airway positioning, suctioning, BLS adjuncts, and advanced airway interventions as indicated.

  2. ⚠ Pediatric <1 Year Critical Positioning:Infants MUST be positioned with head DOWN, below the level of the chest during back blows and chest thrusts. Gravity assists in foreign body expulsion. Never perform abdominal thrusts on infants <1 year.

  3. Obesity and Pregnancy Modifications:⚠ Use CHEST thrusts (not abdominal) for pregnant women at any gestational age and markedly obese adults where abdominal thrusts cannot encircle the abdomen. Position fist midline against sternum, not abdomen.

  4. ⚠ Paramedic Red Dot Skill - Pediatric Intubation:Endotracheal intubation of children <12 years of age is restricted to Paramedic "red dot" skill only. Intermediate providers must contact Medical Control or use SGA for pediatric patients.

  5. No Blind Finger Sweeps:⚠ Never perform blind finger sweeps in any age group. This may push the foreign body deeper into the airway, converting a partial obstruction to a complete obstruction. Use direct visualization (laryngoscopy + forceps) only.

  6. Complete vs. Partial Obstruction:Complete obstruction = unable to speak, cough, or breathe (silent, cyanotic). Requires immediate intervention. Partial obstruction = patient can cough forcefully or make sounds. Encourage coughing; do NOT interfere unless condition worsens.

  7. Post-Obstruction Care:All patients with foreign body removal require transport for evaluation due to risk of airway edema, aspiration pneumonitis, or retained fragments. Monitor closely for delayed complications even if obstruction appears relieved.

  8. Expanding Hematoma / Angioedema:⚠ Patients with rapidly expanding neck hematoma or angioedema (anaphylaxis, ACE inhibitor reaction) may decompensate without warning. Early aggressive airway management BEFORE complete obstruction is critical. Consider standing at head of bed, difficult airway preparation, and early Paramedic intercept.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Toxicology / Medical - Nausea / Vomiting
docs.text: This protocol outlines the assessment and management of patients experiencing persistent nausea or vomiting to prevent dehydration, aspiration, and further clinical deterioration.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Onset and duration of nausea/vomiting
  • Frequency and volume of emesis
  • Presence of blood or coffee-ground material
  • Associated symptoms (pain, fever, diarrhea, vertigo)
  • Recent trauma or head injury
  • Possible ingestions or toxic exposures
  • Current medications
  • Nausea/retching/vomiting
  • Signs of dehydration (poor skin turgor, dry mucous membranes)
  • Tachycardia, hypotension
  • Altered mental status
  • Abdominal tenderness or rigidity
  • Gastrointestinal disorders (gastroenteritis, obstruction, appendicitis)
  • Cardiac ischemia / Myocardial Infarction
  • Head injury / Increased Intracranial Pressure (ICP)
  • Toxicologic / Overdose
  • Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
  • Pregnancy / Ectopic pregnancy
  • Vertigo / Motion sickness

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care • • 2 Place in position of comfort / recovery position if unresponsive; suction as needed • • 3 Refer to Airway and Hypotension protocols as needed • • 4 Ondansetron (Zofran) 4 mg ODT • • 5 Adult: Ondansetron (Zofran) 4 mg IV/IO slow push, IM, or POPediatric: Ondansetron (Zofran) 0.15 mg/kg IV/IO/IM/PO (max 4 mg) • 6 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for additional doses or orders MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ Ondansetron (Zofran) must be administered as a SLOW IV push (over 2-5 minutes) to avoid syncope or other adverse reactions.
  • ⚠ Use caution in patients with known prolonged QT interval, as Ondansetron can cause QT prolongation and potentially lead to Torsades de Pointes.
  • ⚠ Prophylactic use of antiemetics is generally not recommended unless specific clinical indications are present.
  • ⚠ Maximum total dose of Ondansetron is 8 mg for Adults and 0.3 mg/kg for Pediatrics.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Toxicology - Nerve Agents
docs.text: This protocol outlines the pre-hospital management of patients with suspected exposure to chemical nerve agents or organophosphates, characterized by cholinergic crisis and SLUDGE/DUMBBELS symptoms. Rapid identification, decontamination, and administration of antidotes are critical.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Suspected or confirmed exposure to chemical nerve agents
  • Exposure to organophosphate pesticides
  • Mass casualty chemical incidents
  • Onset of symptoms during or shortly after exposure

SLUDGE Symptoms: - Salivation - Lacrimation (tearing) - Urination - Defecation - GI distress - Emesis DUMBBELS Symptoms: - Diarrhea - Urination - Miosis (pinpoint pupils) / Muscle weakness - Bradycardia / Bronchospasm / Bronchorrhea - Emesis - Lacrimation - Salivation / Seizures

  • Ensure scene safety and don appropriate PPE prior to patient contact
  • Identify agent if possible without risking exposure
  • Differentiate from other toxicological emergencies (e.g., opioid overdose, cholinergic crisis from other medications)
  • Patients may require significant amounts of airway suctioning and ventilation support

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care • • 2 Don appropriate PPE; decontaminate patient prior to transport • • 3 Follow Airway Management (Consider early intubation due to copious secretions) • • 4 Refer to Nausea/Vomiting, Seizure, and Hypotension protocols as needed • • 5 DuoDote Autoinjector IM (Adult and Pediatric ≥ 40kg). Repeat in 5 mins up to 3 doses based on symptom severity. • • 6 Atropine sulfate 2 mg IV/IO/IM (Adult and Ped ≥ 40kg); 1 mg IV/IO/IM (Ped < 40kg). Repeat every 5 mins up to 3 doses. (Use if DuoDote unavailable) • 7 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for additional guidance and dosing instructions MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • DuoDote contains Atropine 2.1 mg and Pralidoxime Chloride (2-PAM) 600 mg. • Examples of nerve agents include Sarin (GB), Soman (GD), Tabun (GA), and VX. • ⚠ WARNING: Patients exposed to nerve agents can off-gas and contaminate providers. Decontamination must occur prior to transport. • Atropine is the antidote of choice for organophosphate and nerve agent poisoning. Large and repeated doses may be required to dry secretions. • If using AtroPen autoinjectors for pediatrics, utilize weight-based dosing guidelines.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: This protocol outlines the management of patients suffering from toxic ingestions, overdoses, or environmental poisonings. The primary focus is on immediate airway management, decontamination, removal from the exposure source, and the administration of specific antidotes based on the suspected toxidrome.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Substance ingested, inhaled, or exposed to
  • Time of exposure or ingestion
  • Amount or dose involved
  • Reason for exposure (intentional vs. accidental)
  • Bystander interventions prior to EMS arrival
  • Altered mental status or coma
  • Respiratory depression or arrest
  • Pupillary changes (pinpoint or dilated)
  • Tachycardia, bradycardia, or arrhythmias
  • Nausea, vomiting, SLUDGEM symptoms
  • Hyperthermia or hypothermia
  • Stroke or intracranial hemorrhage
  • Hypoglycemia or DKA
  • Head trauma
  • Sepsis or severe infection
  • Seizure disorder or post-ictal state
  • Hypoxia

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care • • 2 Remove from source / decontaminate patient • • 3 Ensure adequate ventilation / administer supplemental oxygen • • 4 Assess blood glucose / 12-lead ECG • • 5 Opiate Overdose:Administer Naloxone 1-2 mg IN • • 6 Opiate Overdose (Adult):Administer Naloxone 0.5-2 mg IV/IO/IM/INOpiate Overdose (Pediatric):Administer Naloxone 0.1 mg/kg (max 2 mg) IV/IO/IM/IN • 7 Calcium Channel Blocker / Beta Blocker Overdose (Adult):Administer Calcium Chloride 1g IV/IO AND Glucagon 1mg IV/IOCalcium Channel Blocker / Beta Blocker Overdose (Pediatric):Administer Calcium Chloride 20 mg/kg IV/IO AND Glucagon 0.5-1 mg IV/IO • 8 Organophosphate / Cholinergic Exposure (Adult & Pediatric >40kg):Administer Atropine 2 mg IV/IO/IMOrganophosphate / Cholinergic Exposure (Pediatric <40kg):Administer Atropine 1 mg IV/IO/IM • 9 Anti-Cholinergic / TCA Overdose:Initiate active cooling measures • • 10 Anti-Cholinergic / TCA Overdose (Adult):Administer Sodium Bicarbonate 50 mEq IV/IOAnti-Cholinergic / TCA Overdose (Pediatric):Administer Sodium Bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg (max 50 mEq) IV/IO •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ Provider Safety: Ensure scene safety and utilize appropriate PPE. Do not become contaminated while attempting rescue or treatment. • ⚠ Naloxone Administration: Administer only enough Naloxone to restore adequate respiratory drive. Complete reversal may cause acute withdrawal, agitation, or vomiting. • ⚠ CBRNE Exposures: For specific chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear, or explosive exposures, refer to the applicable Poquoson Protocol(s) for CBRNE. • ⚠ Seizures: If the patient experiences a seizure due to the overdose or toxic ingestion, refer to the Seizure protocol. • Tricyclic Antidepressants (TCA): Wide QRS complexes >100ms on the 12-lead ECG in the setting of suspected TCA overdose is an indication for Sodium Bicarbonate administration.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines for managing thermal, chemical, and radiation burns, emphasizing airway assessment, TBSA calculation, and destination routing.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Mechanism of burn (thermal, chemical, radiation)
  • Time of injury
  • Possibility of enclosed space / inhalation injury
  • Exposure to specific chemicals (e.g., Hydrofluoric acid)
  • Associated trauma (e.g., blast, fall, lightning strike)
  • Hoarse voice, stridor, carbonaceous sputum (soot in airway)
  • Facial burns or singed facial hair
  • TBSA Estimation: Rule of Palms (1% = patient's palm) or Rule of 9s (Count only 2nd and 3rd-degree burns)
  • Inhalation injury / Airway compromise
  • Destination Criteria:    - Cardiac arrest / airway compromise -> Closest ED    - Major/multisystem trauma -> Trauma Center (Level I or II)    - Burn Center -> Full thickness, partial thickness > 10% TBSA, circumferential, electrical/chemical, inhalation, or burns to face/hands/feet/genitalia/joints
  • Concomitant trauma or cyanide poisoning

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Stop the burning process safely and remove the patient from the source. Fully expose the injured area and remove constricting items including clothing. • • 2 Assess airway and respiratory effort. Refer to Airway Management and Ventilatory Support protocol. • • 3 Estimate depth of burns and Total Body Surface Area (TBSA) involved using Rule of 9s or patient's palm (1%). Only count partial (2nd degree) and full thickness (3rd degree) burns. • • 4 Apply clean, dry dressings to burns. Prevent loss of body heat and keep the patient warm. • • 5 Establish IV/IO access on a non-burned extremity, if possible (preferably obtain two sites of access). • 6 Hydrofluoric Acid (HF) Burns with Hypotension: Administer Calcium Chloride 1 g IV/IO over 3 mins (Repeat in 5 mins PRN). 7 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control if the most appropriate transport destination is unclear based on criteria. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ Assess for airway / inhalation injury. Consider early definitive airway management for patients with respiratory distress, hoarse voice or stridor, carbonaceous sputum and/or facial burns. Prompt assessment by a qualified RSI paramedic, when available, is preferred. • ⚠ Pre-hospital hypotension solely due to burns < 20% TBSA is unlikely. Assess patients with hypotension/AMS in the setting of limited burns for concomitant trauma or other medical conditions (e.g., cardiac emergency or cyanide poisoning). • ⚠ Hemodynamic instability, airway compromise and/or major traumatic injuries take priority over direct transport to a burn center. Do not bypass a trauma center to transport to a burn center if the patient is unstable. • ⚠ Utilize reverse triage (i.e. treat pulseless/apneic patients first) in the case of lightning strike with multiple patients. Cardiac arrest is likely to be due to airway obstruction or shockable arrhythmia (VF/VT). • Treat road-rash as a burn injury. Assess closely for concomitant blunt trauma. • Hydrofluoric Acid (HF) – binds and rapidly reduces serum calcium leading to circulatory collapse. Treat hypotension with Calcium Chloride 1 g IV/IO over 3 minutes. Repeat in 5 minutes as needed. • Only if necessary (i.e. no other accessible site), obtain IV/IO access through a burned area.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
General - Crush Syndrome & Entrapment
docs.text: Guidelines for managing prolonged entrapment >30 minutes and crush injuries, focusing on the prevention and treatment of hyperkalemia before and after extrication.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Any patient with a trapped/crushed extremity or torso for more than 30 minutes.
  • Prolonged suspension in a harness or prolonged time laying on a hard surface.
  • ECG changes suggestive of hyperkalemia:
  • Peaked T waves
  • Prolonged PR segment (>200 ms)
  • Flattening or loss of P waves
  • Wide QRS complex (Adult > 120 ms, Pediatric > 90 ms)
  • Ectopic beats
  • Arrhythmia (e.g., ventricular tachycardia)
  • Cardiac arrest
  • Risk of hemorrhage vs. fluid resuscitation.
  • Crush syndrome releases lethal amounts of potassium from ischemic muscle.

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol. Refer to Trauma, Hemorrhage Control, and Pain Control protocols as needed. • • 2 If possible, obtain an ECG prior to and after release of the crushed part. • • 3 Administer (when able, initiate prior to release):- ADULT: 0.9% Sodium Chloride 1 L IV/IO in 250 - 500 mL boluses.- PEDIATRIC: 0.9% Sodium Chloride 20 mL/kg IV/IO. • 4 Administer:- ADULT / PEDIATRIC: Continuous Albuterol Sulfate (Proventil) 0.083% solution via nebulizer or BVM. • 5 Administer, preferably via separate lines (when able, initiate prior to release):- ADULT / PEDIATRIC: Sodium Bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg IV/IO slow push. Maximum dose 50 mEq. Followed by 20 mL 0.9% Sodium Chloride flush.For persistent ECG changes suggestive of crush syndrome or cardiac arrest, repeat once. • 6 Administer, preferably via separate lines (when able, initiate prior to release):- ADULT: Calcium Chloride 1 g IV/IO over 3 minutes.- PEDIATRIC: Calcium Chloride 20 mg/kg IV/IO over 3 minutes. Maximum dose 1,000 mg (1 g).For persistent ECG changes suggestive of crush syndrome or cardiac arrest, repeat once. • 7 Assess and continuously monitor cardiac rhythm. • • 8 Extricate patient from entrapment and transport per Administrative Policy – Trauma Field Triage. • • 9 If it is anticipated the patient will not be able to be promptly and safely extricated, contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control at a regional trauma center for additional resources. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ Administer Sodium Bicarbonate and Calcium Chloride in separate lines to prevent precipitation into Calcium Carbonate. If separate IV sites cannot be obtained, flush with at least 20 mL saline between drugs. • ⚠ IV fluids may worsen bleeding. Use clinical judgement when administering fluids to patients with crush injuries, balancing risk of hemorrhage vs benefit in treating crush syndrome. • ⚠ Crush syndrome involves the release of toxins, particularly large amounts of potassium, from skeletal muscle cells following blunt trauma and prolonged soft tissue ischemia. Prehospital treatment is focused on preventing or treating life-threatening arrhythmia due to hyperkalemia. • Crush injuries are not limited to heavy weights applied to extremities for a prolonged time and can occur in a variety of situations such as after prolonged suspension in a harness or following a prolonged time laying on a hard surface. • Continuous Albuterol is given to treat suspected hyperkalemia in the setting of persistent ECG changes. • Widened QRS: Adult > 120 ms, Pediatric > 90 ms.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines for managing electrical contact and lightning injuries, emphasizing scene safety, reverse triage, and specific fluid resuscitation.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Electrical contact
  • Lightning strike
  • Burns
  • Cardiac arrest
  • Arrhythmias
  • Scene safety
  • Reverse triage

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol • • 2 Ensure scene safety / patient not energized. Expose area and remove constricting items. • • 3 Multiple patients: Treat cardiac arrest FIRST (reverse triage) • • 4 Assess airway (manage by least invasive means necessary) • • 5 Estimate TBSA/burn depth (2nd/3rd degree only) • • 6 Apply clean dry dressings. Prevent loss of body heat and keep patient warm. • • 7 Obtain 12-Lead ECG / Monitor rhythm continuously • • 8 Establish IV/IO (x2 preferred on non-burned skin) • 9 SBP < 90 & TBSA > 20%:• Adult: 0.9% Sodium Chloride 500 mL/hr IV/IO• Ped (6-12y): 0.9% Sodium Chloride 250 mL/hr IV/IO• Ped (≤5y): 0.9% Sodium Chloride 125 mL/hr IV/IO •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
1. ⚠ Ensure scene safety and patient is not energized - DO NOT become a second victim.
  2. ⚠ Lightning strikes with multiple victims require REVERSE triage (treat cardiac arrest first).

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
General - Hemorrhage Control
docs.text: Guidelines for managing life-threatening hemorrhage across various anatomical locations including extremity, junctional, and truncal injuries.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Blunt/Penetrating trauma
  • Crush injury
  • Uncontrolled bleeding
  • Pelvic instability
  • Hemodialysis access bleeding
  • Airway/breathing

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Care / Reassess bleeding after treatments performed. • • 2 Head: Apply direct pressure / Hemostatic dressing (avoid eyes/mouth). • • 3 Neck: Apply occlusive dressing / direct pressure (DO NOT pack). • • 4 Extremity: Apply direct pressure/packing ➔ Tourniquet 2-3" above wound ➔ 2nd Tourniquet if needed. • • 5 Junctional (Axilla or femoral): Pack tightly with hemostatic gauze / hold pressure x3 mins. • • 6 Thoracoabdominal: Apply direct pressure / chest seal (DO NOT pack). • • 7 Apply pelvic binder if unstable pelvis suspected. • • 8 Consider pain control per Poquoson Protocol(s). • • 9 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control if hemorrhage cannot be controlled and transport immediately. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ Do NOT release tourniquet once applied.
  • ⚠ Document date/time/location on tourniquet and report to ED.
  • ⚠ Do NOT pack neck or thoracoabdominal wounds.
  • ⚠ Consider pre-positioning tourniquets for crush/suspension injuries before release.
  • Blood pressure cuff is an acceptable alternative if a commercial TQ is unavailable. Improvised tourniquets are unreliable.
  • Hemodialysis access sites may result in life-threatening hemorrhage. Direct pressure/pressure dressing should be used first; followed by tourniquet in the setting of life-threatening hemorrhage.
  • If a tourniquet is placed, an alert patient may require analgesia to manage tourniquet-associated discomfort. See Pain Control protocol.
  • If extremity wound is not accessible, a tourniquet may be placed high on the extremity. Follow manufacturer’s recommendations.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
Overview
docs.text: Guidelines for managing patients struck by lightning, emphasizing scene safety, reverse triage for multiple victims, and treatment of concurrent burns/cardiac arrest.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • MOI: Lightning strike
  • Cardiac arrest
  • Burns
  • Arrhythmias
  • Reverse triage
  • Airway obstruction vs. VF/VT

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol. • • 2 Ensure scene safety and that the patient is not energized (avoid second victim). Fully expose the injured area. Remove constricting items including clothing. • • 3 If multiple patients are present: Treat cardiac arrest FIRST (reverse triage). • • 4 Assess airway and respiratory effort. Ventilate using least invasive means necessary. • • 5 Estimate TBSA and burn depth (only count 2nd and 3rd degree burns). 1% BSA = area of patient's palm. • • 6 Apply clean, dry dressings to burns. Prevent loss of body heat. Keep the patient warm. • • 7 Obtain and transmit a 12-Lead ECG / Monitor rhythm. • • 8 Establish IV/IO access on a non-burned extremity if possible (preferably x2 sites on non-burned skin). Only if necessary, obtain IV/IO access through a burned area. • 9 For an adult patient with SBP less than 90 and TBSA greater than 20%, administer fluid resuscitation:Adult: 0.9% Sodium Chloride at 500 mL/hr IV/IOPediatric (6-12 years): 0.9% Sodium Chloride at 250 mL/hr IV/IOPediatric (≤5 years): 0.9% Sodium Chloride at 125 mL/hr IV/IO •

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text:
• ⚠ Lightning strikes with multiple victims require REVERSE triage - treat pulseless/apneic patients first.
  • ⚠ Cardiac arrest in lightning strikes is likely due to airway obstruction or shockable arrhythmia (VF/VT).
  • ⚠ Ensure scene safety - thunderstorms may still pose a threat.

docs.title: Poquoson Fire and Rescue Patient Care Protocols
General - Trauma
docs.text: Guidelines for the rapid assessment and management of suspected traumatic injuries, emphasizing hemorrhage control, airway management, and rapid transport to a trauma center.

docs.title: Assessment & Criteria
docs.text:
History of Present IllnessSigns & SymptomsDifferential Considerations
  • Mechanism of Injury (MOI)
  • Suspected traumatic injury
  • Hemorrhage
  • Shock
  • Altered mental status
  • Calculate GCS (3-15)
  • Calculate Shock Index (HR/SBP)

docs.title: Interventions
docs.text: # Action / Intervention EMR EMT A I P 1 Follow Universal Patient Care / Initial Patient Contact protocol. • • 2 Ensure scene safety. Extricate/move patient to safety if necessary. • • 3 Maintain cervical spine stabilization/immobilization per Spinal Motion Restriction protocol. • • 4 Assess airway and ventilatory support. Refer to Airway Management and Ventilatory Support procedure. Manage airway by the least invasive means necessary. • • 5 Control hemorrhage. Refer to Hemorrhage Control protocol. • • 6 Assess for shock. Refer to Shock (Hypoperfusion) protocol. • • 7 Obtain IV/IO access. • 8 Fluid Resuscitation:ADULT: 0.9% Sodium chloride IV/IO wide open. (Maximum 2L)PEDIATRIC: 0.9% Sodium chloride 20mL/kg IV/IO. May repeat once if hypotensive. • 9 If considering intubation, assess Shock Index (Heart Rate / Systolic Blood Pressure). If Shock Index is greater than 1, consider resuscitation prior to intubation. Refer to Endotracheal Intubation procedure. • 10 Tension pneumothorax: Decompress the affected side(s). Refer to Chest Decompression procedure. • 11 Traumatic Cardiac Arrest: Refer to Cardiac Arrest: Traumatic protocol. • 12 If the patient meets the trauma triage criteria according to Administrative Policy – Trauma Field Triage, transport immediately to a Level 1 or Level II Trauma Center. Goal scene time: Less than 10 minutes. • • 13 Contact Poquoson OMD / Medical Control for guidance and/or optimal destination determination as needed. MC MC MC MC MC

docs.title: Pearls
docs.text: • ⚠ Do not delay transport to establish IV lines or wait for ALS. • ⚠ Shock Index > 1.0 suggests high risk of post-intubation hypotension/arrest - resuscitate first. • Shock Index = Heart Rate / Systolic Blood Pressure. Higher shock indices correlate with increased risk of post-intubation hypotension and cardiac arrest. • Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) ranges from 3 (Minimum) to 15 (Maximum).
